research_study_list.title: Meta Continual Learning Revisited: Implicitly Enhancing Online Hessian Approximation via Variance Reduction
Meta Continual Learning Revisited: Implicitly Enhancing Online Hessian Approximation via Variance Reduction
Unifying Gradient Estimators for Meta-Reinforcement Learning  via Off-Policy Evaluation
research_study_list.abstract:
Model-agnostic meta-reinforcement learning requires estimating the Hessian
matrix of value functions. This is challenging from an implementation
perspective, as repeatedly differentiating policy gradient estimates may lead
to biased Hessian estimates. In this work, we provide a unifying framework for
estimating higher-order derivatives of value functions, based on off-policy
evaluation. Our framework interprets a number of prior approaches as special
cases and elucidates the bias and variance trade-off of Hessian estimates. This
framework also opens the door to a new family of estimates, which can be easily
implemented with auto-differentiation libraries, and lead to performance gains
in practice.
research_study_list.full_text: Unifying Gradient Estimators for Meta-Reinforcement Learning via Off-Policy Evaluation Yunhao Tang* Columbia University yt2541@columbia.edu Tadashi Kozuno* University of Alberta tadashi.kozuno@gmail.com Mark Rowland DeepMind London markrowland@deepmind.com RГ©mi Munos DeepMind Paris munos@deepmind.com Michal Valko DeepMind Paris valkom@deepmind.com Abstract Model-agnostic meta-reinforcement learning requires estimating the Hessian matrix of value functions. This is challenging from an implementation perspective, as repeatedly differentiating policy gradient estimates may lead to biased Hessian estimates. In this work, we provide a unifying framework for estimating higher- order derivatives of value functions, based on off-policy evaluation. Our framework interprets a number of prior approaches as special cases and elucidates the bias and variance trade-off of Hessian estimates. This framework also opens the door to a new family of estimates, which can be easily implemented with auto-differentiation libraries, and lead to performance gains in practice. We open source the code to reproduce our results1. 1 Introduction Recent years have witnessed the success of reinforcement learning (RL) in challenging domains such as game playing [1], board games [2], and robotics control [3]. However, despite such breakthroughs, state-of-the-art RL algorithms are still plagued by sample inefп¬Ғciency, as training agents requires orders of magnitude more samples than humans would experience to reach similar levels of perfor- mance [1, 2]. One hypothesis on the source of such inefп¬Ғciencies is that standard RL algorithms are not good at leveraging prior knowledge, which implies that whenever presented with a new task, the algorithms must learn from scratch. On the contrary, humans are much better at transferring prior skills to new scenarios, an innate ability arguably obtained through evolution over thousand of years. Meta-reinforcement learning (meta-RL) formalizes the learning and transfer of prior knowledge in RL [4]. The high-level idea is to have an RL agent that interacts with a distribution of environments at meta-training time. The objective is that at meta-testing time, when the agent interacts with previously unseen environments, it can learn much faster than meta-training time. Here, faster learning is measured by the number of new samples needed to achieve a good level of performance. If an agent can achieve good performance at meta-testing time, it embodies the ability to transfer knowledge from prior experiences at meta-training time. meta-RL algorithms can be constructed in many ways, such as those based on recurrent memory [ 5, 6], gradient-based adaptations [ 4], learning loss functions [7вҖ“9], probabilistic inference of context variables [10вҖ“12], online adaptation of hyper-parameters within a single lifetime [13, 14] and so on. Some of these formulations have mathematical connections; see e.g. [15] for an in-depth discussion. 1https://github.com/robintyh1/neurips2021-meta-gradient-offpolicy-evaluation 35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021), Sydney, Australia. arXiv:2106.13125v2  [cs.LG]  3 Nov 2021Table 1: Interpretation of prior work on higher-order derivative estimations as special instances of differentiating off-policy evaluation estimates. Given any off-policy evaluation estimate ЛҶVПҖОё from the second row, we recover higher-order derivative estimates in prior work in the п¬Ғrst row, by differentiating through the estimate вҲҮK Оё ЛҶVПҖОё. Off-policy evaluation estimates STEP -WISE IS [ 26] DOUBLY- ROBUST [27] TAYPO- 1 [28, 29] T AYPO- 2 [29] Prior work DiCE [21] Loaded DiCE [22, 23] LVC [24] Second-order (this work) We focus on gradient-based adaptations [4], where the agent carries out policy gradient updates [16] at both meta-training and meta-testing time. Conceptually, since meta-RL seeks to optimize the way in which the agent adapts itself in face of new environments, it needs to differentiate through the policy gradient update itself. This effectively reduces the meta-RL problem into estimations of Hessian matrices of value functions. Challenges for computing Hessian matrix of value functions. To calculate Hessian matrices (or unbiased estimates thereof) for supervised learning objectives, it sufп¬Ғces to differentiate through gradient estimates, which can be easily implemented with auto-differentiation packages [ 17вҖ“19]. However, it is not the case for value functions in RL. Intuitively, this is because value functions are deп¬Ғned via expectations with respect to distributions that themselves depend on the policy parameters of interest, whereas in supervised learning the expectations are deп¬Ғned with respect to a п¬Ғxed data distribution. As a result, implementations that do not take this into account may lead to estimates [4] whose bias is not properly characterized and might have a negative impact on downstream applications [20]. Motivated by this observation, a number of prior works suggest implementation alternatives that lead to unbiased Hessian estimates [21], with potential variance reduction [22, 23, 20]; or biased estimates with small variance [ 24]. However, different algorithms in this space are motivated and derived in seemingly unrelated ways: for example, [ 21вҖ“23] derive code-level implementations within the general context of stochastic computation graphs [25]. On the other hand, [24] derives the estimates by explicitly analyzing certain terms with potentially high variance, which naturally produces bias in the п¬Ғnal estimate. Due to apparently distinct ways of deriving estimates, it is not immediately clear how all such methods are related, and whether there could be other alternatives. Central contribution. We present a uniп¬Ғed framework for estimating higher-order derivatives of value functions, based on the concept of off-policy evaluation. The main insights are summarized in Table 1, where most aforementioned prior work can be interpreted as special cases of our framework. Our framework has a few advantages: (1) it conceptually uniп¬Ғes a few seemingly unrelated prior methods; (2) it elucidates the bias and variance trade-off of the estimates; (3) it naturally produces new methods based on Taylor expansions of value functions [29]. After a brief background introduction on meta-RL in Section 2, we will discuss the above aspects in detail in Section 3. From an implementation perspective, we will show in Section 4.1 that both the general framework and the new method can be conveniently implemented in auto-differentiation libraries [30, 19], making it amenable in a practical setup. Finally in Section 5, we validate important claims based on this framework with experimental insights. 2 Background We begin with the notation and background on RL and meta-RL. 2.1 Task-based reinforcement learning Consider a Markov decision process (MDP) with state space X and action space A. At time t вүҘ0, the agent takes action at вҲҲA in state xt вҲҲX , receives a reward rt and transitions to a next state xt+1 вҲјp(В·|xt,at). Without loss of generality, we assume a single starting state x0. Here, we assume the reward rt = r(xt,at,g) to be a deterministic function of state-action pair (xt,at) and the task variable g вҲҲG . The task variable g вҲјpG is resampled for every episode. For example, xt вҲҲ Xis the sensory space of a running robot, at вҲҲ Ais the control, g is the 2episodic target direction in which to run and r(xt,at,g) is the speed in direction g. A policy ПҖ : X вҶ’P(A) speciп¬Ғes a distribution over actions at each state. For convenience, we deп¬Ғne the value function VПҖ(x,g) := EПҖ[вҲ‘вҲһ t=0 Оіtr(xt,at,g) |x0 = x] and Q-function QПҖ(x,a,g ) := EПҖ[вҲ‘вҲһ t=0 Оіtr(xt,at,g) |x0 = x,a0 = a]. Without loss of generality, we assume the policy to be smoothly parameterized as ПҖОё with parameter ОёвҲҲRD. We further assume that the MDPs terminate within a п¬Ғnite horizon of H <вҲһunder any policy. 2.2 Gradient-based meta-reinforcement learning The motivation of meta-RL is to identify a policy ПҖОё such that given a task g, after updating the parameter from Оё to ОёвҖІwith a parameter update computed under rewards r(x,a,g ), the resulting policy ПҖОёвҖІ performs well. Formally, deп¬Ғne U(Оё,g) вҲҲRD as a parameter update to Оё, for example, one policy gradient ascent step. Model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) [4] formulates meta-RL as optimizing the value function at the updated policyEgвҲјpG[VПҖОёвҖІ(x,g)] where ОёвҖІ= Оё+ U(Оё,g) is the updated policy. The optimization is with respect to the initial policy parameter Оё. The aim is to п¬Ғnd Оё such that it entails fast learning (or adaptation) to the environment, through the inner loop update operator U(Оё,g), that leads to high-performing updated policy ОёвҖІ. Consider the following problem, max Оё F(Оё) := EgвҲјpG[VПҖОёвҖІ(x0,g)] , ОёвҖІ= Оё+ U(Оё,g). (1) We can decompose the meta-gradient into two terms based on the chain rule, вҲҮОёF(Оё) = EgвҲјpG[вҲҮОёF(Оё,g)] := EgвҲјpG[вҲҮОёОёвҖІвҲҮОёвҖІVПҖОёвҖІ(x0,g)] (2) where вҲҮОёОёвҖІ= I+вҲҮОёU(Оё,g) вҲҲRDГ—D is a matrix, and вҲҮОёвҖІVПҖОёвҖІ(x0,g) is the vanilla policy gradient evaluated at the updated parameter ОёвҖІ[31]. A straightforward way to optimizing Eqn (1) is to carry out the outer loop update ОёвҶҗОё+ ОұвҲҮОёF(Оё) with learning rate Оұ> 0. Policy gradient update. Following MAML [ 4], we focus on policy gradient update where U(Оё,g) = О·вҲҮОёVПҖОё(x0,g) for a п¬Ғxed step size О· >0. The matrix вҲҮОёU(Оё,g) = О·вҲҮ2 ОёVПҖОё(x0,g) is the Hessian of the value function with respect to policy parameters; henceforth, we deп¬Ғne HОё(x0,g) := вҲҮ2 ОёVПҖОё(x0,g) вҲҲRDГ—D. Estimating meta-gradients. Practical algorithms construct stochastic estimates of the meta- gradients using a п¬Ғnite number of samples. In Eqn (2), we decompose the meta-gradients into the multiplication of a Hessian matrix with a policy gradient vector. Given a п¬Ғxed task variable g, a common practice of prior work is to construct the gradient estimate as the product of the Hessian estimate and the policy gradient estimate ЛҶвҲҮF(Оё,g) = (I+ О·ЛҶHОё(x0,g)) ЛҶвҲҮОёвҖІVПҖОёвҖІ(x0,g); see, e.g., [4, 32, 33, 24, 21вҖ“23, 20]. See Algorithm 1 for the full pseudocode for estimating meta-gradients by sampling multiple tasks. Since there is a large literature on constructing accurate estimates to policy gradient (e.g., actor-critic algorithms use baselines for variance reduction [34]), the main challenge consists in estimating the Hessian matrix accurately. Bias of common plug-in estimators. Common practices in meta-RL algorithms rely on the premise that if both Hessian and policy gradient estimates are unbiased, then the meta-gradient estimate is unbiased too. E [ ЛҶHОё(x0,g) ] = HОё(x0,g),E [ ЛҶвҲҮОёвҖІVПҖОёвҖІ(x0,g) ] = вҲҮОёвҖІVПҖОёвҖІ(x0,g) вҮ’E [ ЛҶвҲҮОёF(Оё,g) ] = вҲҮОёF(Оё,g). Unfortunately, this is not true. This is because the two estimates are in general correlated when the sample size is п¬Ғnite, leading to the bias of the overall estimate. We provide further discussions in Appendix A. For the rest of the paper, we follow practices of prior work and focus on the properties of Hessian estimates, leaving a more proper treatment of this bias to future work. 3 Deriving Hessian estimates with off-policy evaluation Since the meta-gradient estimates are computed by averaging over task variables g вҲјpG, in the following, we focus on Hessian estimates at a single state and task variable HОё(x,g) with a п¬Ғxed g. In this section, we also drop the dependency of the value function on g, such that, e.g., VПҖОё(x0) вүЎ VПҖОё(x0,g) and Q(x,a,g ) вүЎQ(x,a). 3Algorithm 1 Pseudocode for computing meta-gradients for the MAML objective for i=1,2...n do Sample task variable gi вҲјpG. Sample B trajectories under policy ПҖОё; Compute B-trajectory policy gradient estimate ЛҶвҲҮОёVПҖОё(x0,gi), update parameter ОёвҖІ= Оё+ ЛҶвҲҮОёVПҖОё(x0,gi). Compute B-trajectory Hessian estimate ЛҶHОё(x0,gi) and an unbiased policy gradient estimate at ОёвҖІ, i.e., ЛҶвҲҮОёвҖІVПҖОё(x0,g). Compute the i-th meta-gradient estimate ЛҶвҲҮF(Оё,gi) = ( I+ О·ЛҶHОё(x0,gi) ) ЛҶвҲҮОёвҖІVПҖОёвҖІ(x0,gi). end for Output averaged meta-gradient estimate 1 n вҲ‘n i=1 ЛҶвҲҮОёF(Оё,gi). 3.1 Off-policy evaluation: maintaining higher-order dependencies on parameters We assume access to data (xt,at,rt)вҲһ t=0 generated under a behavior policy Вө. Off-policy evaluation [26] consists in building estimators ЛҶVПҖОё(x,g) using the behavior data such that ЛҶVПҖОё(x) вүҲVПҖОё(x) for a range of target policies ПҖОё. Note that the estimate ЛҶVПҖОё(x) is a random variable depending on (xt,at,rt)вҲһ t=0, it is also a function of Оё. The approximation ЛҶVПҖОё(x) вүҲVПҖОё(x) implies that ЛҶVПҖОё(x) is indicative of how the value function VПҖОё(x) depends on Оё, and hence maintains the higher-order dependencies on Оё. Throughout, we assume ПҖОё(a|x) >0,Вө(a|x) >0 for all (x,a) вҲҲXГ—A . Example: step-wise importance sampling (IS) estimate. As a concrete example, consider the unbiased step-wise IS estimate ЛҶVПҖОё IS (x0) = вҲ‘вҲһ t=0 Оіt( О sвүӨtПҒОё s ) rt where ПҒОё s := ПҖОё(as|xs)/Вө(as|xs). Since the value function VПҖОё(x) is in general a highly non-linear function of Оё(see discussions in [35]) we see that ЛҶVПҖОё IS (x0) retains such dependencies via the sum of product of IS ratios. 3.2 Warming up: deriving unbiased estimates with variance reduction We start with a general result based on the intuition above: given an estimate ЛҶVПҖОё(x) to VПҖОё(x), we can directly use the mth-order derivative вҲҮm Оё ЛҶVПҖОё(x) вҲҲRDK as an estimate to вҲҮK Оё VПҖОё(x). We introduce two assumptions: (A.1) ЛҶVПҖОё(x) is mth-order differentiable w.r.t.Оё almost surely. (A.2)оө№оө№оө№вҲҮm Оё ЛҶVПҖОё(x) оө№оө№оө№ вҲһ <M for some constant M for the order mof interest. These assumptions are fairly mild; see further details in Appendix C. The following result applies to general unbiased off-policy evaluation estimates. Proposition 3.1. Assume (A.1) and (A.2) are satisп¬Ғed. Further assume we have an estimator ЛҶVПҖОё(x) which is unbiased ( EВө [ ЛҶVПҖОёвҖІ(x) ] = VПҖОёвҖІ(x)) for all ОёвҖІ вҲҲN(Оё) where N(Оё) is some open set that contains Оё. Under some additional mild conditions, the mth-order derivative of the estimate вҲҮm Оё ЛҶVПҖОё(x0) are unbiased estimates to the mth-order derivative of the value function EВө [ вҲҮm Оё ЛҶVПҖОё(x) ] = вҲҮm Оё VПҖОё(x) for mвүҘ1. Doubly-robust estimates. As a special case, we describe the doubly-robust (DR) off-policy eval- uation estimator [36, 27, 37]. Assume we have access to a state-action dependent critic Q(x,a,g ), and we use the notation Q(x,ПҖ(x)) := вҲ‘ aQ(x,a)ПҖ(a|x). The DR estimate is deп¬Ғned recursively as follows, ЛҶVПҖОё DR (xt) = Q(xt,ПҖОё(xt)) + ПҒОё t ( rt + ОіЛҶVПҖОё DR (xt+1) вҲ’Q(xt,at) ) . (3) The DR estimate is unbiased for all ПҖОё and subsumes the step-wise IS estimate as a special case when Q(x,a) вүЎ0. If the critic is properly chosen, e.g., Q(x,a) вүҲQПҖОё(x,a), it can lead to signiп¬Ғcant variance reduction compared to ЛҶVПҖОё IS (x0). By directly differentiating the estimate вҲҮm Оё ЛҶVПҖОё DR (x), we derive estimators for higher-order derivatives of the value function; the result for the gradient in Proposition 3.2 was shown in [38]. Proposition 3.2. Deп¬Ғne ПҖt := ПҖОё(at|xt) and let Оҙt := rt+ ОіЛҶVПҖОё DR (xt+1) вҲ’Q(xt,at) be the sampled temporal difference error at timet. Note that вҲҮОёlog ПҖt вҲҲRD and вҲҮ2 Оёlog ПҖt вҲҲRDГ—D. The estimates 4of higher-order derivatives can be deduced recursively, and in particular form= 1,2, вҲҮОёЛҶVПҖОё DR (xt) = вҲҮОёQ(xt,ПҖОё(xt)) + ПҒОё tОҙtвҲҮОёlog ПҖt + ОіПҒОё tвҲҮОёЛҶVПҖОё DR (xt+1), (4) вҲҮ2 ОёЛҶVПҖОё DR (xt) = ПҒОё tОҙt ( вҲҮ2 Оёlog ПҖt + вҲҮОёlog ПҖtвҲҮОёlog ПҖT t ) + ОіПҒОё tвҲҮОёЛҶVПҖОё DR (xt)вҲҮОёlog ПҖT t + ОіПҒОё tвҲҮОёlog ПҖtвҲҮОёЛҶVПҖОё DR (xt)T + вҲҮ2 ОёQ(xt,ПҖОё(xt)) + ОіПҒОё tвҲҮ2 ОёЛҶVПҖОё DR (xt+1). (5) Bias and variance of Hessian estimates. Proposition 3.1 implies that вҲҮОёЛҶVПҖОё(x0) and вҲҮ2 ОёЛҶVПҖОё(x0) are both unbiased. To analyze the variance, we start with m= 1: note that when on-policy Вө= ПҖt [16], вҲҮОёVПҖОё DR (x0) recovers a form of gradient estimates similar to actor-critic policy gradient with action-dependent baselines [39вҖ“41]; when Q(x,a) is only state dependent, вҲҮОёЛҶVПҖОё DR (x) recovers the common policy gradient estimate with state-dependent baselines [ 34]. As such, the estimates are computed with potential variance reduction due to the critic. Previously, [38] started with the DR estimate and derived a more general result for the on-policy п¬Ғrst-order case. For the Hessian estimate, we expect a similar effect of variance reduction as shown in experiments. Recovering prior work on estimates to higher-order derivatives. When applied to meta-RL, DiCE [21] and its follow-up variants [22, 42] can be seen as special cases ofвҲҮ2 ОёЛҶVПҖОё DR (x) with different choices of the critic Qwhen evaluated on-policyВө= ПҖОё. See Table 1 for the correspondence between prior work and their equivalent formulations under the framework of off-policy evaluation. We will discuss detailed pseudocode in Section 4.1. See also Appendix F for more details. 3.3 Trading-off bias and variance with Taylor expansions Starting from unbiased off-policy evaluation estimates ЛҶVПҖОё(x), we can directly construct unbiased estimates to higher-order derivatives by differentiating the original estimate to obtain вҲҮm Оё ЛҶVПҖОё(x). However, unbiased estimates can have large variance. Though it is possible to reduce variance through the critic, as we will show experimentally, this is not enough to counter the high variance due to products of IS ratios. This leads us to consider trading off bias with variance [43]. Since we postulate that the products of IS ratios lead to high variance, we might seek to control the number of IS ratios in the estimate. We brieп¬Ӯy introduce Taylor expansion policy optimization (TayPO) [29], a natural framework to control for the number of IS ratios in the value estimate. Taylor expansions of value functions. Consider the value function VПҖОё(x0) as a function of ПҖОё. Using the idea of Taylor expansions, we can express VПҖОё(x0) as a sum of polynomials of ПҖОё вҲ’Вө. We start by deп¬Ғning the Kth-order increment as UПҖОё 0 (x0) = VВө(x0), which does not contain any IS ratio (zeroth order); and for K вүҘ1, UПҖОё K (x0) := EВө [ вҲһвҲ‘ t1=0 вҲһвҲ‘ t2=t1+1 ... вҲһвҲ‘ tK=tKвҲ’1+1 ОіtK ( О K i=1(ПҒОё ti вҲ’1) ) QВө(xtK,atK) оҙҷ оҙҳоҙ— оҙҡ ЛҶU ПҖОё K (x0) ] . (6) Intuitively, the Kth-order increment only contains product of KIS ratios. Equation (6) also yields a natural sample-based estimate ЛҶUПҖОё K (x0), which we will discuss later. The Kth-order Taylor expansion is deп¬Ғned as the partial sum of increments VПҖОё K (x0) := вҲ‘K k=0 UПҖОё k (x0). Since VПҖОё K (x0) consists of products of up to KIS ratios, it is effectively the Kth-order Taylor expansion of the value function. The properties are summarized as follows. Proposition 3.3. (Adapted from Theorem 2 of [ 29].) Deп¬Ғne вҲҘПҖвҲ’ВөвҲҘ1 := max x вҲ‘ a|ПҖ(a|x) вҲ’ Вө(a|x)|. Let Cbe a constant and Оө= 1вҲ’Оі Оі . Then the following holds for all K вүҘ0, VПҖОё(x0,g) = VПҖОё K (x0,g)оҙҷ оҙҳоҙ— оҙҡ K-th order expansion + C(вҲҘПҖОё вҲ’ВөвҲҘ1 /Оө)K+1 оҙҷ оҙҳоҙ— оҙҡ residual , (7) If вҲҘПҖОё вҲ’ВөвҲҘ1 <Оө, then VПҖОё(x0) = limKвҶ’вҲһVПҖОё K (x0) = EВө[вҲ‘вҲһ k=0 UПҖОё k (x0)]. 5Sample-based estimates to Taylor expansions of value functions. As shown in Equation (6), ЛҶUПҖОё K (x0) is an unbiased estimate to UПҖОё K (x0). We can naturally deп¬Ғne the sample-based estimate to the Kth-order Taylor expansion, called the TayPO-Kestimate, ЛҶVПҖОё K (x0) := KвҲ‘ k=0 ЛҶUПҖОё k (x0). (8) The expression of ЛҶUПҖОё(x0) contains O(TK) terms if the trajectory is of length T. Please refer to Appendix E for further details on computing the estimates in linear time O(T) with sub-sampling. Note that ЛҶVПҖОё K (x0) is a sample-based estimate whose bias against the value function is controlled by the residual term which decays exponentially when ПҖОё and Вөare close. Similar to how we derived the unbiased estimate вҲҮm Оё ЛҶVПҖОё DR (x), we can differentiate through the TayPO-Kvalue estimate to produce estimates to higher-order derivatives вҲҮm Оё ЛҶVПҖОё K (x0). Bias and variance of Hessian estimates. TayPO-Ktrades-off bias and variance with choices of K. To understand the variance, note that TayPO-Klimits the number multiplicative IS ratios to be K. Though it is difп¬Ғcult to compute the variance, we argue that the variance generally increases with Kas the number of IS ratios increase [26, 27, 44]. We characterize the bias of TayPO-Kas follows. Proposition 3.4. Assume (A.1) and (A.2) hold. Also assume вҲҘПҖОё вҲ’ВөвҲҘ1 вүӨОө= (1 вҲ’Оі)/Оі. For any tensor x, deп¬Ғne вҲҘxвҲҘвҲһ:= maxi|x[i]|. The Kth-order TayPO objective produces the following bias in estimating high-order derivatives, оө№оө№оө№EВө [ вҲҮm Оё ЛҶVПҖОё K ] (x0) вҲ’вҲҮm Оё VПҖОё(x0) оө№оө№оө№ вҲһ вүӨ вҲһвҲ‘ k=K+1 вҲҘвҲҮm Оё UПҖОё k (x0)вҲҘвҲһ. (9) Hence the upper bound for the bias decreases as Kincreases. Importantly, when on-policy Вө= ПҖОё, the Kth-order TayPO objective preserves up toKth-order derivatives for any K вүҘ0, EВө [ вҲҮm Оё ЛҶVПҖОё K (x0) ] = вҲҮm Оё VПҖОё(x0),вҲҖmвүӨK. (10) Though IS ratios ПҒОё t evaluate to 1 when on-policy, they maintain the parameter dependencies in differentiations. As such, higher-order expansions contains products of IS ratios of higher orders, and maintains the high-order dependencies on parameters more accurately. There is a clear trade-off between bias and variance mediated by K. When K increases, the higher-order derivatives are maintained more accurately in expectation, leading to less bias. However, the variance increases too. Recovering prior work as special cases. Recently, [24] proposed a low variance curvature (LVC) Hessian estimate. This estimate is equivalent to the TayPO-Kestimate with K = 1. As also noted by [24], their objective function bears similarities to п¬Ғrst-order policy search algorithms [28, 45, 46], which have in fact been interpreted as п¬Ғrst-order special cases of вҲҮОёЛҶVПҖОё K (x) with K = 1 [29]. Importantly, based on Proposition 3.4, the LVC estimate only maintains the п¬Ғrst-order dependency perfectly but introduces bias when approximating the Hessian, even when on-policy. Limitations. Though the above framework interprets a large number of prior methods as special cases, it has some limitations. For example, the derivation of Hessian estimates based on the DR estimate (Proposition 3.2) involves estimates of the value function ЛҶVПҖОё DR (xt). In practice, when near on-policy ПҖОё вүҲВө, one might replace the DR estimate ЛҶVПҖОё DR by other value function estimate, such as plain cumulative sum of returns or TD( О»), ЛҶVПҖОё TD(О») [22, 23] in Eqn (4). As such, the practical implementation might not strictly adhere to the conceptual framework. In addition, TMAML [20] is not incorporated as part of this framework: we show in Appendix B that the control variate introduced by TMAML in fact biases the overall estimate. 4 From Hessian estimates to meta-gradient estimates A practical desiteratum for meta-gradient estimates is that it can be implemented in a scalable way using auto-differentiation frameworks [30, 19]. Below, we discuss how this can be achieved. 64.1 Auto-differentiating off-policy evaluation estimates for Hessian estimates In practice, we seek Hessian estimates that could be implemented with the help of an established framework, such as auto-differentiation libraries [ 30, 19]. Now we discuss how to conveniently implement ideas discussed in the previous section. Algorithm 2 Example: an off-policy evaluation subroutine for computing the DR estimate Require: Inputs : Trajectory (xt,at,rt)T t=0, target policy ПҖОё, behavior policy Вө, (optional) critic Q. Initialize ЛҶV = Q(xT,ПҖОё(xT),g). for t= T вҲ’1,... 0 do Compute IS ratio ПҒОё t = ПҖОё(at|xt)/Вө(at|xt). Recursion: ЛҶV вҶҗQ(xt,ПҖОё(at),g) + ОіПҒОё t(rt + ОіQ(xt+1,ПҖОё(xt+1),g) вҲ’Q(xt,at)) + ОіПҒОё t ЛҶV. end for Output ЛҶV as an estimate to VПҖОё(x0,g). Auto-differentiating the estimates. We can abstract the off-policy evaluation as a function eval(D,Оё) that takes in some data D and parameter Оё, and outputs an estimate for VПҖОё(x0,g). In particular, Dincludes the trajectories and Оёis input via the policy ПҖОё. As an example, Algorithm 2 shows that for the doubly-robust estimator, the dependency of the estimator onОёis built through the re- cursive computation by eval(D,Оё). In fact, if we implement Algorithm 2 with an auto-differentiation framework (for example, Tensorп¬Ӯow or PyTorch [17вҖ“19]), the higher-order dependency of ЛҶV on Оёis maintained through the computation graph. We can compute the Hessian estimate by directly differentiating through the function outputвҲҮK Оё ЛҶV = вҲҮK Оё eval(D,Оё) вүҲвҲҮK Оё VПҖОё(x0,g). In Appendix F we show how the estimates could be conveniently implemented, as in many deep RL agents (see, e.g., [44, 5, 47, 48]). We also show concrete ways to compute estimates with TayPO-Kbased on [29]. 4.2 Practical implementations of meta-gradient estimates In Equation (2), we write the meta-gradient estimate as a product between an Hessian estimate and a policy gradient. In practice, the meta-gradient estimate is computed via Hessian-vector products to avoid explicitly computing the Hessian estimate of size D2. As such, the meta-gradient estimate could be computed by auto-differentiating through a scalar objective. See Appendix F for details. Bias and variance of meta-gradient estimates. Intuitively, the bias and variance of the Hessian estimates translate into bias and variance of the downstream meta-gradient estimates. Prior work has showed that low variance of meta-gradient estimates lead to faster convergence [49, 50]. However, it is not clear how the bias (such as bias introduced by the Hessian estimates, or the bias due to correlated estimates) theoretically impacts the convergence. We will study empirically the effect of bias and variance in experiments, and leave further theoretical study to future work. 5 Experiments We now carry out several empirical studies to complement the framework developed above. In Section 5.1, we use a tabular example to investigate the bias and variance trade-offs of various estimates, to assess the validity of our theoretical insights. We choose a tabular example because it is straightforward to compute exact higher-order derivatives of value functions and make comparison. In Section 5.2.1 and Section 5.2.2, we apply the new second-order estimate in high-dimensional meta-RL experiments, to assess the potential performance gains in a more practical setup. Though we can compute TayPO-Korder estimates for general K, we focus on K вүӨ2 in experiments. Below, we also address TayPO-1 and TayPO-2 estimates as the п¬Ғrst and second-order estimates respectively. 5.1 Investigating the bias and variance trade-off of different estimates We study the bias and variance trade-off of various estimates using a tabular exmaple. We consider random MDPs with |X| = 10 ,|A|= 5 . The transition matrix of the MDPs are sampled from a Dirichlet distribution. See Appendix G for further details. The policy ПҖОё is parameterized as ПҖОё(a|x) = exp(Оё(x,a))/вҲ‘ bexp(Оё(x,b)). The behavior policy Вөis uniform and Оёis initialized so that Оё(x,a) = log ПҖ(a|x) where ПҖ= (1 вҲ’Оө)Вө+ ОөПҖd for some deterministic policy ПҖd and parameter Оө вҲҲ[0,1]. The hyper-parameter Оөmeasures the off-policyness. In this example, there is no task variable. As performance metrics, we measure the component-wise correlation between the true derivatives вҲҮK Оё VПҖОё(x0) (computed via an oracle) where x0 is a п¬Ғxed starting state, and its estimates 7(a) Gradient - off-policy  (b) Hessian - off-policy  (c) Hessian - sample size  (d) 2-D control: training Figure 1: Fig (a)-(b): performance measure as a function of off-policyness measured by Оө = вҲҘПҖОё вҲ’ВөвҲҘ1. Effect of off-policyness. In Figure 1(a) and (b), we let N = 1000 and show the performance as a function of Оө. When Оөincreases (more off-policy), the accuracy measures of most estimates decrease. This is because off-policyness generally increases both bias and variance (large IS ratios ПҒОё). At this level of sample size, the performance of the second-order estimate degrades more slowly than other estimates, making it the dominating estimate across all values of Оө. We also include truncated DR estimate using min(ПҒОё t,ПҒ) as a baseline inspired by V-trace [44, 47]. The truncation is motivated by controlling the variance of the overall estimate. However, the estimate is heavily biased and does not perform well unless ПҖОё вүҲВө. See Appendix F for more. Consider the case when Оө = 0 and the setup is on-policy. When estimating the policy gradient, almost all estimates converge to the optimal level of accuracy, except for the step-wise IS estimate, where the variance still renders the performance sub-optimal. However, when estimating the Hessian matrix, the п¬Ғrst-order estimate converges to a lower accuracy than both second-order estimate and DR estimate. This validates our theoretical analysis, as both the second-order estimate and DR estimate are unbiased when on-policy. Effect of sample size. Figure 1(c) shows the accuracy measures as a function of sample size N when п¬Ғxing Оө= 0.5. Note that since sample sizes directly inп¬Ӯuence the variance, the results show the variance properties of different estimates. When N is small, the п¬Ғrst-order estimate dominates due to smaller variance; however, whenNincreases, the п¬Ғrst-order estimate is surpassed by the second-order estimate, due to higher bias. For more results and ablation on high-dimensional environments, see Appendix G. 5.2 High-dimensional meta-RL problems Next, we study the practical gains entailed by the second-order estimate in high-dimensional meta-RL problems. We п¬Ғrst introduce a few important algorithmic baselines, and how the second-order estimate is incorporated into a meta-RL algorithm. Baseline algorithms. All baseline algorithms use plain stochastic gradient ascent as the inner loop optimizer: ОёвҖІ = Оё + О·вҲҮОёЛҶVПҖОё(x0,g) where g вҲјpG is a sampled goal and ЛҶVПҖОё(x0,g) is a sample-based estimate of policy gradients averaged over n trajectories. Different algorithms differ in how the inner loop loss is implemented, such that auto-differentiation produces different Hessian estimates: these include TRPO-DiCE [ 21вҖ“23], TRPO-MAML [ 4], TRPO-FMAML [ 4], TRPO-EMAML [32, 33]. Please refer to Appendix G for further details. Note despite the name, TRPO-DiCE baseline uses DR estimate to estimate Hessians. We implement the second-order 8estimate using the proximal meta policy search (PROMP) [ 24] as the base algorithm. By default, PROMP uses the п¬Ғrst-order estimate. Our new algorithm is named PROMP-TayPO-2. 5.2.1 Continuous control in 2D environments Environment. We consider a simple 2-D navigation task introduced in [24]. The state xt is the coordinate of a ball placed inside a room, the action at is the direction in which to push the ball. The goal gвҲҲR4 is an one-hot encoding of which corner of the room contains positive rewards. With 3 adaptation steps, the agent should ideally navigate to the desired corner indicated by g. Training performance. In Figure 1(d), we show the performance curves of various baseline algorithms. Though MAML and EMAML learns quickly during the initial phase of training, they ultimately become unstable. TRPO-DiCE generally underperforms other methods potentially due to bias. On the other hand, FMAML and PROMP both reduce variance at the cost of bias, but they both achieve a slightly lower level of performance compared to PROMP-TayPO- 2. Overall, PROMP-TAyPO-2 achieves much more stable training curves compared to others, potentially owing to the better bias and variance trade-off in the Hessian estimates. 5.2.2 Large scale locomotion experiments Environments. We consider the set of meta-RL tasks based on simulated locomotion in MuJoCo [51]. Across these tasks, the states xt consist of robotic sensory inputs, the actions at are torque controls applied to the robots. The task gis deп¬Ғned per environment: for example, in random goal environment, gвҲҲR2 is a random 2-d goal location that the robot should aim to reach. Experiment setup. We adapt the open source code base by [ 24] and adopt exactly the same experimental setup as [24]. At each iteration, the agent samples n= 40 task variables. For each task, the agent carries out K = 1 adaptation computed based on B = 20 trajectories sampled from the environment, each of length T = 100. See Appendix G for further details on the architecture and other hyper-parameters. We report averaged results with mean Вұstd over 10 seeds. (a) Ant goal  (b) HalfCheetah  (c) Ant  (d) Walker2D Figure 2: Comparison of baselines over a range of simulated locomotion tasks. For task (b)-(d), the goal space consists of 2-d random direction in which the robot should run to obtain positive rewards. For task (a), the goal space is a 2-d location on the plane. Each curve shows the mean Вұstd across 5 seeds. Overall, the second-order estimate achieves marginal gains over the п¬Ғrst-order estimate. Results. The training performance of different algorithmic baselines are shown in Figure 2. Com- paring TRPO-DiCE, TRPO-MAML and PROMP: we see that the results are compatible with those reported in [ 24], where PROMP outperforms TRPO-MAML, while TRPO-DiCE generally per- forms the worst potentially due to high variance in the gradient estimates. As a side observation, TRPO-FMAML generally underperforms TRPO-MAML, which implies the necessity of carrying out approximations to the Hessian matrix beyond the identity matrix. PROMP-TayPO- 2 slightly outperforms PROMP in a few occasions, where the new algorithm achieves slightly faster learning speed and sometimes higher п¬Ғnal performance. However, overall, we see that the empirical gains are marginal. This implies that under the default setup of these meta-RL experiments, the variance might be a major factor in gradient estimates, and the п¬Ғrst-order estimate is near optimal compared to other estimates. See Appendix G for additional experiments. 6 Conclusion We have uniп¬Ғed a number of important prior work on meta-gradient estimations for model-agnostic meta-RL. Our analysis entails the derivations of prior methods based on the unifying framework of 9differentiating through off-policy evaluation estimates. This framework provides a principled way to reason about the bias and variance in the higher-order derivative estimates of value functions, and opens the door to a new family of estimates based on novel off-policy evaluation estimates. As an important example, we have theoretically and empirically studied the properties of the family of TayPO-based estimates. It is worth noting that this framework further suggests any future advances in off-policy evaluation could be conveniently imported into potential improvements in meta-gradient estimates. As future work, we hope to see the applications of such principled estimates in broader meta-RL applications. Acknowledgement. Accounting for this difference, we have EПҖОё [ вҲҮ2 ОёJ ] = EПҖОё [ 2 HвҲ’1вҲ‘ t=0 вҲҮОёlog ПҖОё(at|xt) (вҲҮОёlog ПҖОё(at|xt))T b(xt) ] , which in general does not evaluate to zero. As a result, we showed that TMAML [20] might achieve variance reduction by introducing baselines to the Hessian estimates of DiCE [21], but at the cost of bias. 14C Further discussions on the assumptions In this section, we examine if the assumptions (A.1) and (A.2) are realistic. Both assumptions depend on particular functional form of the estimates ЛҶVПҖОё. In general, we might assume that the estimates do not explicitly depend on Оё. Instead, they depend on Оё via ПҖОё. This involves a two-stage parameterization: ОёвҶҰвҶ’ПҖОё and ПҖОё вҶҰвҶ’ЛҶVПҖОё. The two assumptions (A.1) and (A.2) can be realized by imposing constraints on these two parameterizations, as well as the off-policyness of ПҖОё relative to Вө, as discussed below. Off-policyness. In general, we might want to assume the ratios are bounded ПҒОё t <R for constant some R <вҲһ. This is a common assumption. In our framework, we usually apply the estimates within a trust region optimization algorithm [45, 46], this naturally proIS duces a bound on the ratios. Parameterization Оё вҶҰвҶ’ ПҖОё. We seek parameterizations where вҲҮОёПҒОё t are bounded. This can be achieved by bounding ПҒОё t and вҲҮОёlog ПҖОё(a|x). If we consider a tabular representation with softmax parameterization ПҖ(a|x) вҲқ exp(Оё(x,a)). Under this parameterization, we can show |вҲҮm Оё log ПҖОё(a|x)|<M are bounded for all (x,a) and all Оё. Parameterization ПҖОё вҶҰвҶ’ ЛҶVПҖОё. We want this parameterization to be sufп¬Ғciently smooth. In the examples we consider, TayPO-Kclearly satisп¬Ғes this assumption because it is a polynomial in ПҖОё. For DR, this assumption is satisп¬Ғed when the MDP terminates within a п¬Ғnite horizon of H <вҲһ, such that the estimator contains polynomials of ПҖОё with order at most H. As such, the estimate takes linear time to compute, at the cost of potentially larger variance. 17F High-level code for implementations of Hessian estimates and meta-gradients Below, we introduce a few important details on how to convert off-policy evaluation estimates into Hessian estimates, with the help of auto-diff. F.1 Implementing Hessian estimates with off-policy evaluation subroutines In Figure 3, we show a high-level JAX implementation of estimating Hessians using off-policy evaluia- tion subroutines. The pseudocode assumes access to some well-established off-policy evaluation functions, as are commonly implemented in off-policy RL algorithms such as ACER [5], Retrace [44], IMPALA [47], R2D2 [48] and so on. The function needs to be written in auto-differentiation libraries, such that after the computations, value function estimatesвҖҷ parameter dependencies are naturally maintained. Then this pipeline could be directly implemented as part of a meta-RL algorithm. Figure 3: JAX-based high-level code for the implementation of Hessian estimates. We can easily con- vert any established trajectory-based off-policy evaluation subroutine into estimates of Hessian matrix, by auto-differentiating through the estimates. This can be implemented in any auto-differentiation frameworks. F.2 Examples of off-policy evaluation estimates The following off-policy evaluation estimates can be abstracted as functions that take as input: a partial trajectory (xt,at,rt)T t=0 of length T + 1, the target policy ПҖОё and a behavior policy Вө. Optionally, the function could also take as input some critic function Q. We detail how to compute certain estimates below. DR estimates. In Algorithm 2, we provided the pseudocode for computing DR estimates. The step-wise IS estimates could be computed as a special case by setting Q= 0. TayPO-1 estimates. See Algorithm 3 for details. TayPO-2 estimates. F.3 Implementing meta-RL estimates To implement meta-RL estimates in a auto-differentiation framework, the aim is to construct a single scalar objective, the auto-differentiation of which produces an estimate to the meta-gradient. Let ЛҶVinner(Оё,D) be an inner loop objective one can use to construct the inner loop adaptation steps. In this case, ЛҶVinner(Оё,D) can take as input: the parameter Оёand some data D. Here, for example, the data Dmight consist of sampled trajectories and other hyper-parameters such as discount factors. In our paper, this objective can be replaced by any off-policy evaluation estimates. The updated parameter is computed as: ОёвҖІ= Оё+ О·вҲҮОёЛҶVinner(Оё,D). The meta objective is deп¬Ғned as the value function, or equivalently some outer loop objective ЛҶVouter(Оё,D) that also takes as input the parameter Оёand some data D. The overall objective can be deп¬Ғned as: ЛҶVouter ( Оё+ О·вҲҮОёЛҶVinner(Оё,D) ) . Auto-differentiaing through the above objective can produce estimates to meta-gradients. This objective is also easy to implement in auto-differentiation frameworks. 19G Experiment Below, we introduce further details in the experiments. G.1 Tabular MDP MDPs. These MDPs have |X| = 10 states and |A|= 5 actions. The transition probabilities p(В·|x,a) are generated from independent Dirichlet distributions with parameter (Оұ,...Оұ) вҲҲR|X|. Here, we set Оұ= 0.001. The discount factor is Оі = 0.8 for all problems. Setups. The policy ПҖОё is parameterized as ПҖОё(a|x) = exp(Оё(x,a))/вҲ‘ bexp(Оё(x,b)). The behav- ior policy Вөis uniform and Оёis set such that Оё(x,a) = log ПҖ(a|x) where ПҖ = (1 вҲ’Оө)Вө+ ОөПҖd for some deterministic policy ПҖd and parameter ОөвҲҲ[0,1]. Experiments. In each experiment, we generate a random MDP and initialize the policy. The agent colelcts N trajectories of length T = 20 (such that ОіT вүҲ0) from a п¬Ғxed initial state x0. We then compute gradient and Hessian estimates of the initial state VПҖОё(x0) by directly diffrentiating through various N-trajectory off-policy evaluation estimates: вҲҮm Оё ЛҶVПҖОё(x0) for m= 1,2. We also calculate the ground truth gradient and Hessian using transition probabilities of the MDPs. Accuracy measure. Given an estimate xвҲҲRL and a ground truth value yвҲҲRL, we measure the accuracy between the two tensors as: Acc(x,y) := xTyвҲҡ xTx вҲҡ yTy . Note that this measure is bounded between [вҲ’1,1]. This advantage of this measure is that it neglects the absolute scales of the tensors, i.e., if x = ky for some scalar k Мё= 0 , then Acc(x,y) = 1 . This metric is used in a number of prior work [ 21вҖ“23] and is potentially a more suitable measure of accuracy given that in large scale experiments, downstream applications typically use adaptive optimizers. Further results: effect of sample size. In Figure 1(a), we п¬Ғx the level of off-policyness Оө= 0.5 and show the estimates as a function sample size N. As N increases, the accuracy measures of most estimates increase. Intuitively, this is because the variance decreases while the bias is not impacted by the sample size. Comparing the step-wise IS estimate with the DR estimate, we see that the DR estimate generally performs better due to variance reduction. This is consistent with п¬Ғndings in prior work [22, 23]). Further, it is worth noting the п¬Ғrst-order estimate performs quite well when the N is small, outperforming the DR estimate. This implies the importance of controlling the number of step-wise IS ratios for further variance reduction. However, as N increases, the performance of the п¬Ғrst-order estimate does not improve as much compared to other alternatives, and is п¬Ғnally surpassed by the DR estimate mainly due to bigger bias. Consider the second-order estimate. When N is small, the second-order estimate slightly underper- forms the п¬Ғrst-order estimate. This is expected because at small sample sizes, the variance dominates. However, as N increases, its performance quickly tops across all different estimates, including the DR estimate. Overall, we expect the second-order estimate to achieve a better bias-variance trade-off, especially in the medium data regime. This should be more signiп¬Ғcant in large-scale setups where estimation horizons are longer and variance dominates further. G.2 Large scale experiments In large scale experiments, including the continuous 2-D navigation environments and simulated locomotion environments, we adopt the following setups. Code base. We adopt the code base released by [ 24]. We make minimal changes to the code base, such that the second-order estimate is comparable to other algorithms under the established experimental setups. For missing hyper-parameters, please refer to the code base for further details. Importantly, note that in the original code base as well as the paper [ 24], the authors suggest the default learning rate of Оұ = 10вҲ’3, which we п¬Ғnd tends to destabilize learning. Instead, we use Оұ= 10вҲ’4, which works more stably. Computational resources. All high-dimensional experiments are run on a computer cluster with multiple CPUs. Each separate experiment is run with 12 CPUs as actors for data collection and parallel computations of parameter updates. The run time for each experiment is on average 36 hours per experiment. For small experiments, we run them on a single laptop machine with 8 CPUs. 20(a) Gradient - sample size Figure 4: Performance measure as a function of sample size . Each plot shows the accuracy measure between the estimates and the ground truth. Overall, the second-order estimate achieves a better bias and variance trade-off. Here, the plot shows results for estimating gradients. Agent details. The agent adopts a MLP architecture with [64,64] hidden units. The agent takes in a state xand outputs a Gaussian policy aвҲјN(ВөОё(x),Пғ2(x)) where ВөОё,ПғОё are parameterized by the neural networks. The agent collects samples n= 40 goals at each iteration to construct meta-gradient estimates; the inner loop adaptation is computed with step size О· = 0.1. Inner loop adaptations are computed with B = 20 trajectories each of length T = 100. All outer loop optimizers use the learning rate Оұ= 10вҲ’4. Algorithmic details. The PROMP and PROMO-TayPO-2 enforces a soft trust region constraint through clipping ПҒОё t = clip(ПҒОё t,1 вҲ’Оө,1 + Оө), where by default Оө= 0.3. The PPO optimizers take 5 gradient steps during each iteration. All outer loop gradient based optimizers use Adam optimizers [52]. Summary of baselines. The baselines include the following: TRPO-MAML uses TRPO as the outer loop optimizer [45] and the biased MAML implementation [4]; TRPO-FMAML, which is short for п¬Ғrst-order MAML, approximates the Hessian matrix by an identity matrix [4]; TRPO-EMAML augments the MAML loss function by an exploration bonus term, which effectively corrects for the bias introduced by vanilla MAML [32, 33]; TRPO-DiCE, which uses the DR estimate to implement the inner loop update, such that the Hessian estimates are unbiased [21вҖ“23]. Closely related to our new algorithm is the proximal meta policy search (PROMP) [24], which uses PPO as the outer loop optimizer [46] and the п¬Ғrst-order estimate (LVC estimate) as the inner loop loss function [28, 29]. Our new algorithm is called PROMP-TayPO-2, which is a combination of PROMP and TayPO-2. The only difference between PROMP and PROMP-TayPO-2 is that the inner loop loss function is now implemented with the second-order estimate to alleviate the bias introduced by the п¬Ғrst-order term. Practical implementations of second-order estimates. We denote ЛҶV1 as value function estimate based on the п¬Ғrst-order approximation; and let ЛҶV2 be the value function estimate. In practice, the second-order estimate we implement is a mixture between the two estimates with ОІ вҲҲ[0,1] ЛҶVОІ = (1 вҲ’ОІ)ЛҶV1 + ОІЛҶV2. This overall estimate is a convex combination of the two estimates. By moving ОІ = 0 to ОІ = 1, we interpolate between the п¬Ғrst-order and the second-order estimate. Throughout the large-scale experiments we п¬Ғnd ОІ = 0.3 to work the best. We also show in the next section the sensitivity of performance to ОІon 2-Dcontrol environments. 21(a) 2-D control: second-order coefп¬Ғcient  (b) 2-D control: off-policy Figure 5: Ablation study: (a) second-order coefп¬Ғcients; (b) off-policyness. The above two plots show the sensitivity of the п¬Ғrst and second-order estimate to hyper-parameters, for 2-Dcontrol. The second-order estimate is generally more robust. All curves are averaged over 10 runs. G.3 Ablation study Sensitivity to the second-order coefп¬Ғcient on 2- D control environments. In Figure 5(a), we show the sensitivity of the performance to the mixing coefп¬Ғcient ОІ вҲҲ[0,1]. Note that when ОІ = 0, the estimate is exactly the п¬Ғrst-order estimate; whenОІ = 1, the estimate recovers the full second-order estimate. We see that on the 2-Dcontrol environment, as we increase ОІfrom 0 to 1, the performance stably improves. When ОІ вүҲ0.9, it seems that the performance reaches a plateau, indicating that ОІ = 0.9 potentially achieves the best bias and variance trade-off between the two extremes. Sensitivity to off-policyness on 2- Dcontrol environments. In Figure 5(b), we study the sensi- tivity of algorithms to off-policyness in high-d setups. In PROMP, the policies are optimized with behavior policy Вө, whose distance against the target policy is constrained by a trust region. The trust region is enforced softly via a clipping coefп¬Ғcient Оө(see Appendix G or [24] for details). When Оө increases, the effective level of off-policyness increases. We see that as Оөincreases, both п¬Ғrst and second-order estimates degrade in performance. However, the second-order estimates perform more robustly, as similarly suggested in the toy example. 22

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: The paper presents a unifying framework for estimating higher-order derivatives (specifically Hessians) of value functions in model-agnostic meta-reinforcement learning. This framework is based on off-policy evaluation (OPE) and demonstrates that several prior estimators like DiCE, Loaded DiCE, and LVC are special cases. It clarifies the bias-variance trade-off in these estimates and introduces a new family of estimators based on Taylor Expansion Policy Optimization (TayPO), which can be easily implemented using auto-differentiation libraries.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: The core methodology is to derive Hessian estimates by differentiating through an off-policy evaluation (OPE) estimate of the value function, вҲҮВІОё ЛҶVПҖОё. Two primary families of OPE estimates are used: 1) Unbiased estimates derived from Doubly-Robust (DR) estimators, which can reduce variance via a learned critic function. 2) Biased estimates derived from K-th order Taylor Expansion Policy Optimization (TayPO-K), which explicitly trade off bias for lower variance by limiting the number of importance sampling (IS) ratios to K.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: The experiments were conducted on three types of environments: 1) Tabular random MDPs (|X|=10, |A|=5) to analyze the bias-variance trade-off using component-wise correlation as the accuracy metric. 2) A 2D continuous control navigation task. 3) Large-scale MuJoCo locomotion tasks (Ant, HalfCheetah, Walker2D). The proposed method, PROMP-TayPO-2, was benchmarked against TRPO-MAML, TRPO-FMAML, TRPO-EMAML, TRPO-DiCE, and PROMP. Performance was measured by training curves (average return) over multiple seeds.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: The paper notes several limitations: 1) Practical implementations may deviate from the conceptual framework, such as by replacing parts of the Doubly-Robust estimate with other value estimates (e.g., TD(О»)). 2) The framework does not incorporate the TMAML algorithm, which is shown to introduce bias. 3) The analysis focuses on the properties of the Hessian estimate itself, leaving a rigorous treatment of the bias arising from the correlation between the Hessian and policy gradient estimates to future work.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: The paper suggests several directions for future research: 1) A more rigorous theoretical analysis of the bias caused by the correlation between Hessian and policy gradient estimates. 2) Investigating the theoretical impact of bias in Hessian estimates on the convergence of meta-RL algorithms. 3) Leveraging future advances in the field of off-policy evaluation to create improved meta-gradient estimators. 4) Applying the principled estimators developed in this framework to a broader range of meta-RL applications.

research_study_list.title: Efficient Continual Finite-Sum Minimization
research_study_list.abstract:
Given a sequence of functions $f_1,\ldots,f_n$ with $f_i:\mathcal{D}\mapsto
\mathbb{R}$, finite-sum minimization seeks a point ${x}^\star \in \mathcal{D}$
minimizing $\sum_{j=1}^n f_j(x)/n$. In this work, we propose a key twist into
the finite-sum minimization, dubbed as continual finite-sum minimization, that
asks for a sequence of points ${x}_1^\star,\ldots,{x}_n^\star \in \mathcal{D}$
such that each ${x}^\star_i \in \mathcal{D}$ minimizes the prefix-sum
$\sum_{j=1}^if_j(x)/i$. Assuming that each prefix-sum is strongly convex, we
develop a first-order continual stochastic variance reduction gradient method
($\mathrm{CSVRG}$) producing an $\epsilon$-optimal sequence with
$\mathcal{\tilde{O}}(n/\epsilon^{1/3} + 1/\sqrt{\epsilon})$ overall first-order
oracles (FO). An FO corresponds to the computation of a single gradient $\nabla
f_j(x)$ at a given $x \in \mathcal{D}$ for some $j \in [n]$. Our approach
significantly improves upon the $\mathcal{O}(n/\epsilon)$ FOs that
$\mathrm{StochasticGradientDescent}$ requires and the $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \log
(1/\epsilon))$ FOs that state-of-the-art variance reduction methods such as
$\mathrm{Katyusha}$ require. We also prove that there is no natural first-order
method with $\mathcal{O}\left(n/\epsilon^\alpha\right)$ gradient complexity for
$\alpha < 1/4$, establishing that the first-order complexity of our method is
nearly tight.
research_study_list.full_text: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024 EFFICIENT CONTINUAL FINITE -SUM MINIMIZATION Ioannis MavrothalassitisвҲ—, Stratis SkoulakisвҲ—, Leello Tadesse Dadi, Volkan Cevher LIONS, Гүcole Polytechnique FГ©dГ©rale de Lausanne {ioannis.mavrothalassitis, efstratios.skoulakis, leello.dadi, volkan.cevher}@epfl.ch ABSTRACT Given a sequence of functions f1, . . . , fn with fi : D 7вҶ’R, finite-sum minimiza- tion seeks a point xвӢҶ вҲҲ Dminimizing Pn j=1 fj(x)/n. In this work, we propose a key twist into the finite-sum minimization, dubbed ascontinual finite-sum minimiza- tion, that asks for a sequence of points xвӢҶ 1, . . . , xвӢҶ n вҲҲ Dsuch that each xвӢҶ i вҲҲ Dmin- imizes the prefix-sum Pi j=1 fj(x)/i. Assuming that each prefix-sum is strongly convex, we develop a first-order continual stochastic variance reduction gradient method (CSVRG ) producing an Пө-optimal sequence with ЛңO(n/Пө1/3 + 1/вҲҡПө) over- all first-order oracles (FO). An FO corresponds to the computation of a single gradient вҲҮfj(x) at a given x вҲҲ Dfor some j вҲҲ [n]. Our approach significantly improves upon the O(n/Пө) FOs that StochasticGradientDescent requires and the O(n2 log(1/Пө)) FOs that state-of-the-art variance reduction methods such as Katyusha require. We also prove that there is no natural first-order method with O (n/ПөОұ) gradient complexity for Оұ <1/4, establishing that the first-order com- plexity of our method is nearly tight. 1 I NTRODUCTION Given n data points describing a desired input and output relationship of a model, a cornerstone task in supervised machine learning (ML) is selecting the modelвҖҷs parameters enforcing data fidelity. In optimization terms, this task corresponds to minimizing an objective with the finite-sum structure. Finite-sum minimization: Given a sequence of functions f1, . . . , fn with fi : D 7вҶ’R, let xвӢҶ вҲҲ arg minx g(x) := Pn i=1 fi(x)/n. Given an accuracy target Пө >0, finite-sum minimization seeks an approximate solution ЛҶx, such that: g(ЛҶx) вҲ’ g(xвӢҶ) вүӨ Пө (1) we call such an ЛҶx an Пө-accurate solution. If ЛҶx is random then (1) takes the form E[g(ЛҶx)] вҲ’ g(xвӢҶ) вүӨ Пө. In contemporary machine learning applications, n can be in the order of billions, which makes it clear that methods tackling finite-sum minimization must efficiently scale with n and the accuracy Пө >0. First-order methods have been the standard choice for tackling (1) due to their efficiency and practical behavior. The complexity of a first-order method is captured through the notion of overall first-order oracles (FOs) where a first-order oracle corresponds to the computation of a single gradient вҲҮfi(x) for some i вҲҲ [n] and a point x вҲҲ D. In case each function fi is strongly convex,Stochastic Gradient Descent requires O(1/Пө) FOs Robbins and Monro (1951); Kiefer and Wolfowitz (1952) so as to solve (1). Over the last years, the so-called variance reduction methods are able to solve strongly convex finite-sum problems with O(n log(1/Пө)) FOs Nguyen et al. (2017b); Johnson and Zhang (2013); Xiao and Zhang (2014); Defazio et al. (2014); Roux et al. (2012b); Allen-Zhu (2017). Continual Finite-Sum Minimization: In many modern applications new data constantly arrives over time. Hence it is important that a model is constantly updated so as to perform equally well both on the past and the new data Castro et al. (2018); Rosenfeld and Tsotsos (2018); Hersche et al. (2022). Unfortunately updating the model only with respect to the new data can lead to a vast deterioration of its performance over the past data. The latter phenomenon is known in the context of continual learning as catastrophic forgetting Castro et al. (2018); Goodfellow et al. (2014); Kirkpatrick et al. (2017); McCloskey and Cohen (1989). At the same time, retraining the model from scratch using вҲ—Equal contribution 1 arXiv:2406.04731v1  [math.OC]  7 Jun 2024Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024 both past and new data comes with a huge computational burden. Motivated by the above, we study a twist of (1), called continual finite-sum minimization. Continual Finite-Sum Minimization: Given a sequence of functions f1, . . . , fn with fi : D 7вҶ’R, let xвӢҶ i вҲҲ arg minxвҲҲD gi(x) := Pi j=1 fj(x)/i for all i вҲҲ [n]. Given an accuracy target Пө > 0, continual finite-sum minimization seeks a sequence of approximate solutions ЛҶx1, . . . ,ЛҶxn вҲҲ D, such that: gi(ЛҶxi) вүӨ gi(xвӢҶ i ) + Пө for each i вҲҲ [n] (2) We call such a sequence an Пө-optimal sequence. If xвӢҶ i is random then (2) takes the form E[gi(ЛҶxi)] вҲ’ gi(xвӢҶ i ) вүӨ Пө Remark 1. Throughout the paper we assume that each function fi(В·) is strongly convex, smooth and that D is convex and bounded. See Section 2 for the exact definitions. Notice that fi(В·) can capture a new data point meaning that (2) guarantees that, the model (parameter- ized by ЛҶxi) is well-fitted in all of the data seen so far for each stage i вҲҲ [n]. As already discussed, since n can be in the order of millions, it is important to design first-order methods for continual finite-sum minimization that efficiently scale with n and Пө >0 with respect to overall FOs. A first attempt for tackling (2) is via existing first-order methods for finite-sum minimization. As discussed above StochasticGradientDescent can guarantee accuracy Пө >0 for (1) using O(1/Пө). As a result, at each stage i вҲҲ [n] one could perform O(1/Пө) stochastic gradient decent steps to guarantee accuracy Пө >0. However the latter approach would require overall O(n/Пө) FOs. On the other hand one could use a VR methods to select ЛҶxi вҲҲ Dat each stage i вҲҲ [n]. As already mentioned such methods require O(i log(1/Пө)) FOs to guarantee accuracy Пө >0 at stage i вҲҲ [n]. Thus the naive use of a VR method such as SVRG Johnson and Zhang (2013); Xiao and Zhang (2014), SAGA Defazio et al. (2014), SARAH Nguyen et al. (2017b) and Katyusha Allen-Zhu (2017) would require overall O(n2 log(1/Пө)) FOs. We remark that for large values ofn and even mediocre accuracy Пө >0 both O(n/Пө) and especially O(n2 log(1/Пө)) are prohibitely large. As a result, the following question arises: Question 1. Are there first-order methods for continual finite-sum minimization whose required number of FOs scales more efficiently with respect to n and Пө >0? Our Contribution The main contribution of this work is the design of a first-order method for Con- tinual finite-sum minimization, called CSVRG, with overall ЛңO � n/Пө1/3 + logn/вҲҡПө  FO complexity. The latter significantly improves upon the O(n/Пө) FO complexity of SGD and the O(n2 log(1/Пө)) complexity of variance reduction methods. As a naive baseline, we also present a sparse version of SGD tailored (2) with O(log n/Пө2) FO complexity. The latter improves on the n dependence of SGD with the cost of a worse dependence on 1/Пө. Since variance-reduction methods such as Katyusha are able to achieve O(n2 log(1/Пө)) FO com- plexity. A natural question is whether there exists a first-order method maintaining the log(1/Пө) dependence with a subquadratic dependence on n, O � n2вҲ’Оұ log(1/Пө)  . We prove that the latter goal cannot be achieved for a wide class of first-order methods that we call natural first-order methods. More precisely, we establish that there is no natural first-order method for (2) requiring O(n2вҲ’Оұ log(1/Пө)) FOs for any Оұ >0. We also establish that there is no natural first-order method for (2) requiring ЛңO (n/ПөОұ) FOs for any Оұ <1/4. The latter lower bound implies that the ЛңO � n/Пө1/3 FO complexity of CSVRG is close to being tight. Table 1 summarizes our results. Comparing the methods for Оҳ(1/n) accuracy According to the level of accuracy Пө >0 that (2) requires, different methods might be more efficient than others. We remark that for the accuracy regime of Пө = O(1/n) all previous first-order methods require O(n2) FOs while CSVRG requires only ЛңO(n4/3) FOs! We remark that the accuracy regime Пө = O(1/n) for (2) is of particular interest and has been the initial motivation of this work. The reason for the latter lies on the generalization bounds in the strongly convex case. More precisely, the statistical error of empirical risk minimization in the strongly convex case is Оҳ(1/n) Shalev-Shwartz et al. (2010). Thus the accuracy of the respective finite-sum problem should be selected as Пө = O(1/n) so as to match the unavoidable statistical error (see Bottou and Bousquet (2007) for the respective discussion). For accuracy Пө = Оҳ(1/n), a very 2Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024 Пө-accuracy at each stage i вҲҲ [n] Method Number of FOs StochasticGradientDescent O( 1 Вө n/Пө) SVRG/SAGA/SARAH O  n2 log(1/Пө) + L Вө В· n log(1/Пө)  Katyusha O  n2 log(1/Пө) + q L Вө В· n3/2 log(1/Пө)  SGD-sparse (naive baseline) O  |D|G3 Вө 1/Пө2  CSVRG (this work) O  L2/3G2/3 Вө В· (n log n)/Пө1/3 + L2G Вө5/2 В· log n/вҲҡПө  Lower Bound (this work) в„Ұ � n2 log(1/Пө)  Lower Bound (this work) в„Ұ � n/Пө1/4 Table 1: Number of FOs for the strongly convex case for an Пө-accurate solution for each stage i вҲҲ [n]. interesting open question is bridging the gap between the O(n4/3) FO complexity of CSVRG and the в„Ұ(n5/4) FO complexity that our lower bound suggests (setting Пө = 1/n and Оұ = 1/4). Our Techniques As already discussed the naive use of a VR method at each stage i вҲҲ [n] results in overall O(n2 log(1/Пө)) FOs. A first approach in order to alleviate the latter phenomenon is to sparsely use such a method across the n stages and in most intermediate stages compute ЛҶxi вҲҲ D via O(1) FOs using a stochastic gradient estimator. However both the sparsity level for using a VR method as well as the stochastic gradient estimator are crucial design choices for establishing accuracy Пө >0 at each stage i вҲҲ [n]. Our first-order method (CSVRG) adopts a variance-reduction pipeline along the above lines.CSVRG (Algorithm 1) maintains a direction ЛңвҲҮi that at stage i вҲҲ [n] tries to approximate Pi k=1 вҲҮfk(ЛҶxiвҲ’1)/i. Since computing the latter direction at each stage i вҲҲ [n] would lead to в„Ұ(n2) FOs, CSVRG вҖў Sets ЛңвҲҮi вҶҗ Pi k=1 вҲҮfk(ЛҶxi)/i only in a very sparse sub-sequence of stages (i FOs) вҖў Sets ЛңвҲҮi вҶҗ (1 вҲ’ 1 i ) ЛңвҲҮi + 1 i вҲҮfi(ЛҶxprev) in most stages (1 FO) In order to output an Пө-accurate point ЛҶxi вҲҲ Dat stage i вҲҲ [n], we initialize an internal subroutine (Algorithm 2) to the previous output ЛҶxiвҲ’1 вҲҲ Dand perform Ti stochastic gradient descent steps using the following novel gradient estimator вҲҮt i вҶҗ  1 вҲ’ 1 i  вҲҮfut(xt i) вҲ’ вҲҮfut(ЛҶxprev) + ЛңвҲҮiвҲ’1  + 1 i вҲҮfi(xt i) where index ut is selected uniformly at random in [i вҲ’ 1] and prev is the latest stage в„“ вүӨ i вҲ’ 1 at which ЛңвҲҮв„“ = Pв„“ k=1 вҲҮfk(ЛҶxв„“)/в„“. Notice that the estimator вҲҮt i requires only 3 FOs. Combining all the latter, we establish that by an appropriate parametrization on the sparsity of the sub-sequence at which ЛңвҲҮв„“ = Pв„“ k=1 вҲҮfk(ЛҶxв„“)/в„“ and an appropriate selection of iteration Ti, our first-order method CSVRG requires overall O(n log n/Пө1/3) FOs. 1.1 R ELATED WORK Apart from the close relation of our work with the long line of research on variance reduction methods (see also Appendix A for a detailed discussion), the motivation of our work also relates with the line of research in incremental learning, the goal of which is adapting a model to new information, data or tasks without forgetting the ones that it was trained on earlier. The phenomenon of losing sight of old information is called catastrophic forgetting Castro et al. (2018); Goodfellow et al. (2014); Kirkpatrick et al. (2017); McCloskey and Cohen (1989); Mermillod et al. (2013) and it is one of the main challenges of incremental learning. There have been three main empirical approaches to tackle catastrophic forgetting, regularization based Nguyen et al. (2017a), memory based Tulving (1985), architecture based Yoon et al. (2017), as well as combination of the above Sodhani et al. (2020). 3Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024 2 P RELIMINARIES In this section we introduce some basic definitions and notation. We denote with Unif(1, . . . , n) the uniform distribution over {1, . . . , n} and [n] := {1, . . . , n}. Definition 1 (Strong Convexity). A differentiable function f : D 7вҶ’R is Вө-strongly convex in D if and only if for all x, yвҲҲ D f(x) вүҘ f(y) + вҲҮf(y)вҠӨ(x вҲ’ y) + Вө 2 вҲҘx вҲ’ yвҲҘ2 (3) In Problem (2) we make the assumption that D is convex and compact. We denote with |D| the diameter of D, |D| = maxx,yвҲҲD вҲҘx вҲ’ yвҲҘ2. The compactness of D also provides us with the property that each fi : D 7вҶ’R is also G-Lipschitz and L-smooth. More precisely, вҖў |f(x) вҲ’ f(y)| вүӨG В· вҲҘx вҲ’ yвҲҘ (G-Lipschtiz) вҖў вҲҘвҲҮf(x) вҲ’ вҲҮf(y)вҲҘ вүӨL В· вҲҘx вҲ’ yвҲҘ (L-smooth) where G = maxxвҲҲD вҲҘвҲҮf(x)вҲҘ2 and L = maxxвҲҲD вҲҮ2f(x)  2. To simplify notation we denote as gi(x) the prefix-function of stage i вҲҲ [n], gi(x) := iX j=1 fj(x)/i (4) We denote with xвӢҶ i вҲҲ Dthe minimizer of the function gi(x), xвӢҶ i := arg minxвҲҲD gi(x). We denote the projection of x вҲҲ Rd to the set D as О D (x) := arg minzвҲҲD вҲҘx вҲ’ zвҲҘ2. For a set S вҠҶ Rd, we denote О D(S) := {О D(x) : for all x вҲҲ S}. Throughout the paper we assume that each function fi(В·) in continual finite-sum minimization (2) is Вө-strongly convex, L-smooth and G-Lipschitz. 3 O UR RESULTS We first state our main result establishing the existence of a first-order method for continual finite-sum minimization (2) with ЛңO � n/Пө1/3 + logn/вҲҡПө  FO complexity. Theorem 1. There exists a first-order method, CSVRG (Algorithm 1), for continual finite-sum minimization (2) with O  L2/3G2/3 Вө В· n log n Пө1/3 + L2G Вө5/2 В· log nвҲҡПө  FO complexity. As already mentioned, our method admits ЛңO � n/Пө1/3 + 1/вҲҡПө  first-order complexity, significantly improving upon the O(n/Пө) of SGD and the O � n2 log(1/Пө)  of VR methods. The description of our method (Algorithm 1) as well as the main steps for establishing Theorem 1 are presented in Section 4. In Theorem 2 we present the formal guarantees of the naive baseline result that is a sparse variant of SGD for continual finite-sum minimization (2) with O(1/Пө2) FO complexity. Theorem 2. There exists a first-order method, SGD-sparse (Algorithm 4), for continual finite-sum minimization (2) with O  G3|D| log n ВөПө2  FO complexity. Algorithm 4 as well as the proof of Theorem 2 are presented in Appendix I. Lower Bounds for Natural First-Order Methods In view of the above, the question that nat- urally arises is whether there exists a method for continual finite-sum minimization (2) with O(n2вҲ’Оұ log(1/Пө)) FO complexity. Unfortunately the latter goal cannot be met for a wide class of first-order methods, that we call natural. The formal definition of natural first-order method is presented in Section 3.1. To this end, we remark that methods such that GD, SGD, SVRG, SAGA, SARAH, Katyusha and CSVRG (Algorithm 1) lie in the above class. Theorem 3. For any Оұ > 0, there is no natural first-order method for Problem (2) with O � n2вҲ’Оұ log(1/Пө)  FO complexity. Moreover for any Оұ < 1/4, there is no natural first-order method for continual finite-sum minimization (2) with O (n/ПөОұ) FO complexity. Due to space limitations the proof of Theorem 3 is presented in Appendix H. Remark 2. The ЛңO(n/Пө1/3 + 1/вҲҡПө) FO complexity of CSVRG is close to theв„Ұ(n/Пө1/4) lower bound. 4Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024 3.1 N ATURAL FIRST -ORDER METHODS In this section we provide the formal definition of natural first-order method for which our lower bound stated in Theorem 3 applies. Before doing so we present the some definitions characterizing general first-order methods. At an intuitive level a first-order method for continual finite-sum minimization (2) determines ЛҶxi вҲҲ D by only requiring first-order access to the functions f1, . . . , fi. Specifically at each stage i вҲҲ [n], a first-order method makes queries of the form {вҲҮfj(x) for j вүӨ i} and uses this information to determine ЛҶxi вҲҲ D. The latter intuitive description is formalized in Definition 2. Definition 2. Let A be a first-order method for continual finite-sum minimization (2). At each stage i вҲҲ [n], вҖў ЛҶx0 вҲҲ Ddenotes the initial point of A and ЛҶxi вҲҲ Ddenotes the output of A at stage i вҲҲ [n]. вҖў xt i вҲҲ Ddenotes the intermediate point of A at round t вүҘ 1 of stage i вҲҲ [n] and Ti вҲҲ N the number of iterations during stage i вҲҲ [n]. вҖў At each round t вҲҲ [Ti] of stage i вҲҲ [n], A performs a set of first-order oracles denotes Qt i. вҖ“ Each first-order oracle q вҲҲ Qt i admits the form q = (qvalue, qindex) where qvalue вҲҲ D denotes the queried point and qindex вүӨ i denotes the index of the queried function. вҖ“ A computes the gradients {вҲҮfqindex (qvalue) for all q вҲҲ Qt i} and uses this information to determine xt+1 i вҲҲ D. The FO complexity of A is thus P iвҲҲ[n] PTi t=1 |Qt i|. Example 1. Thus in terms of Definition 2 the first-order oracles of GD at round t вҲҲ Ti are Qt i = { � xtвҲ’1 i , 1  , . . . , � xtвҲ’1 i , i  }. In Definition 3 we formally define the notion of a natural first-order method. In simple terms a first-order method is called natural if during each stage i вҲҲ [n] it only computes FOs of the form вҲҮfj(x) where x вҲҲ Dis previously generated point and j вүӨ i. Definition 3. A first-order method A is called natural iff at each step t вҲҲ [Ti] of stage i вҲҲ [n], вҖў For any query q вҲҲ Qt i, qindex вүӨ i and qvalue вҲҲ PPtвҲ’1 i where PPtвҲ’1 i := (вҲӘmвүӨiвҲ’1 вҲӘП„вүӨTm xП„ m) вҲӘ (вҲӘП„вүӨtвҲ’1xП„ i ) вҲӘ ЛҶx0 (PPtвҲ’1 i stands for previous points) вҖў xt i вҲҲ О D(S) where S is the linear span of  вҲӘqвҲҲQt i вҲҮfqindex (qvalue)  вҲӘ PPtвҲ’1 i . вҖў ЛҶxi вҲҲ О D(S) where S is the linear span of PPTi i . Remark 3. Definition 3 also captures randomized first-order methods such as SGD by considering Qt i being a random set. Natural first-order methods is the straightforward extension of linear-span methods Bubeck (2015); Nesterov (2014) in the context of continual finite-sum minimization (2). Linear-span methods require that for any first-order oracle вҲҮf(x) computed at iteration t, x вҲҲ {x0, x1, . . . , xtвҲ’1}. Moreover xt is required to lie in the linear span of the previous points and the computed gradients. Linear-span methods capture all natural first-order optimization methods and have been used to establish the well-known в„Ұ(1/вҲҡПө) and в„Ұ p L/Вө log(1/Пө)  respectively for the convex and strongly convex case (see Section 3.5 in Bubeck (2015)). 4 CSVRG AND CONVERGENCE RESULTS In this section we present our first-order method CSVRG that is able to achieve the guarantees established in Theorem 1. The role of ЛңвҲҮi: In order to keep the variance of the estimator вҲҮt i low, we would ideally want ЛңвҲҮiвҲ’1 := вҲҮgiвҲ’1(ЛҶxiвҲ’1). CSVRG/SGD/SGD- sparse perform roughly 4% of the FOs of Katyusha/SVRG. 6 E XPERIMENTS We experimentally evaluate the methods (SGD,SGD-sparse, Katyusha, SVRG and CSVRG) on a ridge regression task. Given some dataset (ai, bi)n i=1 вҲҲ Rd Г— R, at each stage i вҲҲ [n] we consider the the finite sum objective gi(x) := Pi j=1(aвҠӨ j x вҲ’ bj)2/i + О» вҲҘxвҲҘ2 2 with О» = 10вҲ’3. We choose the latter setting so as to be able to compute the exact optimal solution at each stage i вҲҲ [n]. For our experiments we use the datasets found in the LIBSVM packageChang and Lin (2011) for which we report our results. At each stage i вҲҲ [n], we reveal a new data point (ai, bi). In all of our experiments we run CSVRG with Оұ = 0 .3 and Ti = 100 . The inner iterations of SGD and SGD-sparse are appropriately selected so that their overall FO calls match the FO calls of CSVRG. At each stage i вҲҲ [n] we run Katyusha Allen-Zhu (2017) and SVRG Johnson and Zhang (2013) on the prefix-sum function gi(x) with 10 outer iterations and 100 inner iterations. In Table 2 we present the error achieved by each method at each stage i вҲҲ [n] and the overall number of FOs until the respective stage. As our experimental evaluations reveal, CSVRG nicely interpolates between SGD/SGD-sparse and Katyusha/SVRG. More precisely, CSVRG achieves a significantly smaller suboptimality gap than SGD/SGD-sparse with the same number of FOs while it achieves a comparable one with Katyusha/SVRG with way fewer FOs. In Appendix K.2 we present further experimental evaluations as well as the exact parameters used for each method. 7 C ONCLUSIONS In this work we introduce continual finite-sum optimization, a tweak of standard finite-sum minimiza- tion, that given a sequence of functions asks for a sequence of Пө-accurate solutions for the respective prefix-sum function. For the strongly convex case we propose a first-order method with ЛңO(n/Пө1/3) FO complexity. This significantly improves upon the O(n/Пө) FOs that StochasticGradientDescent requires and upon the O(n2 log(1/Пө)) FOs that VR methods require. We additionally prove that no natural method can achieve O � n2вҲ’Оұ log(1/Пө)  FO complexity for any Оұ >0 and O (n/ПөОұ) FO complexity for Оұ <1/4. 9Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024 8 A CKNOWLEDGEMENTS Authors acknowledge the constructive feedback of reviewers and the work of ICLRвҖҷ24 program and area chairs. Limitations: The theoretical guarantees of our proposed method are restricted to the strongly convex case. Extending our results beyond the strong convexity assumptions is a very promising research direction. Reproducibility Statement In the appendix we have included the formal proofs of all the theorems and lemmas provided in the main part of the paper. In order to facilitate the reproducibility of our theoretical results, for each theorem we have created a separate self-contained section presenting its proof. Concerning the experimental evaluations of our work, we provide the code used in our experiments as well as the selected parameters in each of the presented methods.
