llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: The paper addresses the inefficiency and scalability issues of Linearized Laplace Approximation (LLA) for Bayesian deep learning. Current LLA methods rely on approximations like Kronecker-factoring or diagonal matrices, which can degrade performance. The main contribution is a new method called accElerated Linearized Laplace Approximation (ELLA), which uses a NystrГ¶m approximation of the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) to scale LLA to large models like Vision Transformers. ELLA avoids explicit computation of large GGN/Jacobian matrices, offers theoretical guarantees on its approximation error, and identifies an 'overfitting issue' in LLA where uncertainty diminishes too rapidly with more data.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: ELLA is based on the connection between LLA and Gaussian Processes (GPs), where the predictive covariance is a function of the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK). The core technique is to approximate the NTK using the NystrГ¶m method. This involves sampling M data points to find the top-K eigenvalues and eigenfunctions of the NTK, which creates a low-rank approximation of the kernel. This reduces the problem of inverting a large (NC x NC) matrix to inverting a much smaller (K x K) matrix. The implementation is made efficient by using forward mode automatic differentiation (fwAD) to compute the necessary Jacobian-vector products (JVPs) without storing the full Jacobian.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: The method was evaluated on a 1D synthetic regression task and image classification benchmarks including CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. Out-of-distribution (OOD) performance was tested on CIFAR-10 corruptions, ImageNet corruptions, and a combined CIFAR-10+SVHN dataset. The architectures used include MLPs, various ResNets (ResNet-20, ResNet-50, etc.), and Vision Transformers (ViT-B). ELLA was compared against baselines such as MAP, Mean-Field Variational Inference (MFVI-BF), and several LLA variants (last-layer LLA*, LLA-KFAC, LLA-Diag). Performance was measured using test accuracy, Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL), and Expected Calibration Error (ECE).
llm_extracted_info.limitations: The paper identifies an 'overfitting issue' where ELLA's predictive uncertainty shrinks excessively as the training dataset size increases, for which the proposed solution is a heuristic (early stopping). The method's performance depends on the choice of hyperparameters M (number of NystrГ¶m samples) and K (number of eigenpairs). On ImageNet, while ELLA improved calibration (ECE), another method (MFVI-BF) achieved better accuracy and NLL, suggesting that ELLA's fixed MAP estimate might be suboptimal for sharp posteriors. The theoretical analysis is simplified by assuming the number of samples M equals the number of eigenpairs K.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: The paper suggests extending ELLA to estimate the model evidence, which could be used for model selection tasks. Another proposed direction is to use the estimated model evidence to automatically select the hyperparameter K (the number of eigenpairs). The developed NystrГ¶m approximation for NTKs is also noted as potentially useful for other future applications of NTKs.

research_study_list.title: Accelerated Linearized Laplace Approximation for Bayesian Deep Learning
research_study_list.abstract:
Laplace approximation (LA) and its linearized variant (LLA) enable effortless
adaptation of pretrained deep neural networks to Bayesian neural networks. The
generalized Gauss-Newton (GGN) approximation is typically introduced to improve
their tractability. However, LA and LLA are still confronted with non-trivial
inefficiency issues and should rely on Kronecker-factored, diagonal, or even
last-layer approximate GGN matrices in practical use. These approximations are
likely to harm the fidelity of learning outcomes. To tackle this issue,
inspired by the connections between LLA and neural tangent kernels (NTKs), we
develop a Nystrom approximation to NTKs to accelerate LLA. Our method benefits
from the capability of popular deep learning libraries for forward mode
automatic differentiation, and enjoys reassuring theoretical guarantees.
Extensive studies reflect the merits of the proposed method in aspects of both
scalability and performance. Our method can even scale up to architectures like
vision transformers. We also offer valuable ablation studies to diagnose our
method. Code is available at \url{https://github.com/thudzj/ELLA}.
research_study_list.full_text: Accelerated Linearized Laplace Approximation for Bayesian Deep Learning Zhijie Deng1 2, Feng Zhou2 3, Jun Zhu2 вҲ— 1 Qing Yuan Research Institute, Shanghai Jiao Tong University 2 Dept. of Comp. Sci. & Tech., BNRist Center, THU-Bosch Joint ML Center, Tsinghua University 3 Center for Applied Statistics, School of Statistics, Renmin University of China zhijied@sjtu.edu.cn, feng.zhou@ruc.edu.cn, dcszj@tsinghua.edu.cn Abstract Laplace approximation (LA) and its linearized variant (LLA) enable effortless adaptation of pretrained deep neural networks to Bayesian neural networks. The generalized Gauss-Newton (GGN) approximation is typically introduced to im- prove their tractability. However, LA and LLA are still confronted with non-trivial inefп¬Ғciency issues and should rely on Kronecker-factored, diagonal, or even last- layer approximate GGN matrices in practical use. These approximations are likely to harm the п¬Ғdelity of learning outcomes. To tackle this issue, inspired by the connections between LLA and neural tangent kernels (NTKs), we develop a Nys- trГ¶m approximation to NTKs to accelerate LLA. Our method beneп¬Ғts from the capability of popular deep learning libraries for forward mode automatic differ- entiation, and enjoys reassuring theoretical guarantees. Extensive studies reп¬Ӯect the merits of the proposed method in aspects of both scalability and performance. Our method can even scale up to architectures like vision transformers. We also offer valuable ablation studies to diagnose our method. Code is available at https://github.com/thudzj/ELLA. 1 Introduction Deep neural networks (DNNs) excel at modeling deterministic relationships and have becomede facto solutions for diverse pattern recognition problems [18, 54]. However, DNNs fall short in reasoning about model uncertainty [1] and suffer from poor calibration [17]. These issues are intolerable in risk-sensitive scenarios like self-driving [27], healthcare [34], п¬Ғnance [25], etc. Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs) have emerged as effective prescriptions to these pathologies [37, 21, 44, 16]. They usually proceed by estimating the posteriors over high-dimensional NN parameters. Due to some intractable integrals, diverse approximate inference methods have been applied to learning BNNs, spanning variational inference (VI) [1, 36], Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) [55, 4], Laplace approximation (LA) [38, 50, 30], etc. LA has recently gained unprecedented attention because its post-hoc nature nicely suits with the pretraining-п¬Ғnetuning fashion in deep learning (DL). LA approximates the posterior with a Gaussian around its maximum, whose mean and covariance are the maximum a posteriori (MAP) and the inversion of the Hessian respectively. It is a common practice to approximate the Hessian with the generalized Gauss-Newton (GGN) matrix [40] to make the whole workп¬Ӯow more tractable. Linearized LA (LLA) [14, 22] applies LA to the п¬Ғrst-order approximation of the NN of concern. Immer et al. [22] argue that LLA is more sensible than LA in the presence of GGN approximation; вҲ—Corresponding author. Majority of work completed while Zhijie Deng was at Tsinghua University. 36th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2022). arXiv:2210.12642v1  [cs.LG]  23 Oct 2022ELLA  LLA  LLA-KFAC  LLA-Diag  LLA * Figure 1: 1-D regression on y= sin 2x+ Пө,Пө вҲјN(0,0.2). Red dots, central blue curves, and shaded regions refer to the training data, mean predictions, and uncertainty respectively. The model is a pretrained multilayer perceptron (MLP) with 3 hidden layers. As shown, the predictive uncertainty of ELLA is on par with or better than the competitors such as LLA with KFAC approximation (LLA-KFAC), LLA with diagonal approximation (LLA-Diag), and last-layer LLA (LLAвҲ—). LLA can perform on par with or better than popular alternatives on various uncertainty quantiп¬Ғcation (UQ) tasks [14, 6, 7]. The Laplace library [6] further substantially advances LLAвҖҷs applicability, making it a simple and competing baseline for Bayesian DL. The practical adoption of LA and LLA actually entails further approximations on top of GGN. E.g., when using Laplace to process a pretrained ResNet [18], practitioners are recommended to resort to a Kronecker-factored (KFAC) [40] or diagonal approximation of the full GGN matrix for tractability. An orthogonal tactic is to apply LA/LLA to only NNsвҖҷ last layer [30]. Yet, the approximation errors in these cases can hardly be identiп¬Ғed, signiп¬Ғcantly undermining the п¬Ғdelity of the learning outcomes. This paper aims at scaling LLA up to make probabilistic predictions in a more assurable way. We п¬Ғrst revisit the inherent connections between Neural Tangent Kernels (NTKs) [24] and LLA [28, 22], and п¬Ғnd that, if we can approximate the NTKs with the inner product of some low-dimensional vector representations of the data, LLA can be considerably accelerated. Given this п¬Ғnding, we propose to adapt the NystrГ¶m method to approximate the NTKs of multi-output NNs, and advocate leveraging forward mode automatic differentiation (fwAD) to efп¬Ғciently compute the involved Jacobian-vector products (JVPs). The resultant accElerated LLA (ELLA) preserves the principal structures of vanilla LLA yet without explicitly computing/storing the costly GGN/Jacobian matrices for the training data. WhatвҖҷs more, we theoretically analyze the approximation error between the predictive of ELLA and that of vanilla LLA, and п¬Ғnd that it deceases rapidly as the NystrГ¶m approximation becomes accurate. We perform extensive studies to show that ELLA can be a low-cost and effective baseline for Bayesian DL. We п¬Ғrst describe how to specify the hyperparameters of ELLA, and use an illustrative regression task to demonstrate the effectiveness of ELLA (see Figure 1). We then experiment on standard image classiп¬Ғcation benchmarks to exhibit the superiority of ELLA over competing baselines in aspects of both performance and scalability. We further show that ELLA can even scale up to modern architectures like vision transformers (ViTs) [11]. 2 Background Consider a learning problem on D= (X,Y) = {(xi,yi)}N i=1, where xi вҲҲX and yi вҲҲRC (e.g., regression) or {0,1}C (e.g., classiп¬Ғcation) refer to observations and targets respectively. The advance in machine learning suggests using an NN gОё(В·) : XвҶ’ RC with parameters ОёвҲҲRP for data п¬Ғtting. Despite well-performing, regularly trained NNs only capture the most likely interpretation for the data, thus miss the ability to reason about uncertainty and are prone to overп¬Ғtting and overconп¬Ғdence. BNNs [37, 21, 44] characterize model uncertainty by probabilistic principle and can holistically rep- resent all likely interpretations. Typically, BNNs impose a priorp(Оё) on NN parameters and chase the Bayesian posterior p(Оё|D) = p(D|Оё)p(Оё)/p(D) where p(D|Оё) = вҲҸ ip(yi|xi,Оё) = вҲҸ ip(yi|gОё(xi)). Analytical estimation is usually intractable due to NNsвҖҷ high nonlinearity. Thereby, BNN methods usually п¬Ғnd a surrogate of the true posterior q(Оё) вүҲp(Оё|D) via approximate inference methods like variational inference (VI) [1, 20, 36, 62, 29], Laplace approximation (LA) [38, 50], Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) [55, 4, 63], particle-optimization based variational inference (POVI) [35], etc. BNNs predict for new data xвҲ— by posterior predictive p(y|xвҲ—,D) = Ep(Оё|D)p(y|xвҲ—,Оё) вүҲ Eq(Оё)p(y|xвҲ—,Оё) вүҲ1 S вҲ‘ S s=1 p(y|gОёs (xвҲ—)) where Оёs вҲјq(Оё) are i.i.d. Monte Carlo (MC) samples. 22.1 Laplace Approximation and Its Linearized Variant Typically, LA builds a Gaussian approximate posterior in the form ofq(Оё) = N(Оё; ЛҶОё,ОЈ),where ЛҶОёde- notes the MAP solution, i.e., ЛҶОё= arg maxОёlog p(D|Оё) + logp(Оё), and ОЈ is the inversion of the Hes- sian of the negative log posterior w.r.t. parameters, i.e., ОЈвҲ’1 = вҲ’вҲҮ2 ОёОё(log p(D|Оё) + logp(Оё))|Оё=ЛҶОё. Without loss of generality, we base the following discussion on the isotropic Gaussian prior p(Оё) = N(Оё; 0,Пғ2 0IP)1, so вҲ’вҲҮ2 ОёОёlog p(Оё)|Оё=ЛҶОё equals to IP/Пғ2 0. Due to the intractability of the Hessian for NNs with massive parameters, it is a common practice to use the symmetric positive and semi-deп¬Ғnite (SPSD) GGN matrix as a workaround, i.e., ОЈвҲ’1 = вҲ‘ i JЛҶОё(xi)вҠӨОӣ(xi,yi)JЛҶОё(xi) + IP /Пғ2 0, (1) where JЛҶОё(x) вүң вҲҮОёgОё(x)|Оё=ЛҶОё and Оӣ(x,y) вүң вҲ’вҲҮ2 gglog p(y|g)|g=gЛҶОё(x). When concatenating {JЛҶОё(xi) вҲҲ RCГ—P}N i=1 as a big matrix JЛҶОё,X вҲҲ RNCГ—P and organizing {Оӣ(xi,yi) вҲҲRCГ—C}N i=1 as a block-diagonal matrix ОӣX,Y вҲҲRNCГ—NC, we have: ОЈ = [ JЛҶОё,X вҠӨОӣX,YJЛҶОё,X + IP /Пғ2 0 ]вҲ’1 . (2) Yet, LA suffers from underп¬Ғtting [33]. This is probably because GGN approximation implicitly turns the original model gОё(x) into a linear oneglin Оё (x) = gЛҶОё(x)+JЛҶОё(x)(ОёвҲ’ЛҶОё) but gОё(x) is still leveraged to make prediction. I.e., there exists a shift between posterior inference and prediction [ 22]. To mitigate this issue, a proposal is to predict withglin Оё (x), giving rise to linearized LA (LLA) [14, 28, 22]. LA and LLA nicely п¬Ғt the pretraining-п¬Ғnetuning fashion in DL вҖ“ they can be post-hoc applied to pretrained models where only the GGN matrices require to be estimated and further inverted. By LA and LLA, practitioners can adapt off-the-shelf high performing DNNs to BNNs easily. LLA has revealed strong results on diverse UQ problems [14, 6, 7]. The Laplace library [6] further advances LLAвҖҷs applicability, and evidences LLA is competitive to popular alternatives [63, 32, 39]. Scalability issue The GGN matrix of size P Г—P is still unamenable in modern DL scenarios, so further approximations sparsifying it are always introduced. The diagonal and KFAC [ 40] approximations are commonly adopted ones [ 50, 62], where only a diagonal or block-diagonal structure of the original GNN matrix is preserved. An orthogonal tactic is to concern only a subspace of the high-dimensional parameter space (e.g., the parameter space of the last layer [30]) to reduce the scale of the GGN matrix. However, these strategies sacriп¬Ғce the п¬Ғdelity of the learning outcomes as the approximation errors in these cases can hardly be theoretically measured. To this end, we develop accElerated Linearized Laplace Approximation (ELLA) to push the limit of LLA in a more assurable way. 3 Methodology In this section, we п¬Ғrst revisit the relation of LLA to Gaussian processes (GPs) and Neural Tangent Kernels (NTKs) [24]. After that, we reveal how to accelerate LLA by kernel approximation. Based on these п¬Ғndings, we develop an efп¬Ғcient implementation of ELLA using the NystrГ¶m method [58]. 3.1 The Gaussian Process View of LLA Integrating q(Оё) = N(Оё; ЛҶОё,ОЈ) with the linear model glin Оё (x) actually gives rise to a function-space approximate posterior [14, 28, 22] in the form of q(f) = GP(f|gЛҶОё(x),ОәLLA(x,xвҖІ)) with ОәLLA(x,xвҖІ) вүң JЛҶОё(x)ОЈJЛҶОё(xвҖІ) вҠӨ . (3) By Woodbury matrix identity [60], we have: ОЈ = [ JЛҶОё,X вҠӨОӣX,YJЛҶОё,X + IP /Пғ2 0 ]вҲ’1 = Пғ2 0 ( IP вҲ’JЛҶОё,X вҠӨ[ОӣвҲ’1 X,Y/Пғ2 0 + JЛҶОё,XJЛҶОё,X вҠӨ]вҲ’1JЛҶОё,X ) . (4) 1IP refers to the identity matrix of size P Г—P. 3It follows that ОәLLA(x,xвҖІ) = Пғ2 0 ( ОәNTK(x,xвҖІ) вҲ’ОәNTK(x,X)[ОӣвҲ’1 X,Y/Пғ2 0 + ОәNTK(X,X)]вҲ’1ОәNTK(X,xвҖІ) ) , (5) where ОәNTK(x,xвҖІ) вүң JЛҶОё(x)JЛҶОё(xвҖІ)вҠӨdenotes the neural tangent kernel (NTK) [24] corresponding to gЛҶОё(x). Note that ОәNTK(x,xвҖІ) is a matrix-valued kernel, with values in the space of CГ—Cmatrices. The main challenge then turns into the computation and inversion of the gram matrix ОәNTK(X,X) of size NC Г—NC. When either N or Cis large, the estimation of ОәLLA still suffers from inefп¬Ғciency issue. To address this, existing work [22] assumes independence among the C output dimensions to cast q(f) into Cindependent GPs following [51], and randomly subsample M вүӘN data points to form a cheap substitute for the original gram matrix. Despite effective in some cases, these approximations are heuristic, lacking a clear theoretical foundation. 3.2 Scale Up LLA by Kernel Approximation We show that if we can approximate ОәNTK(x,xвҖІ) with the inner product of some explicit CГ—K- dimensional representations of the data, i.e., ОәNTK(x,xвҖІ) вүҲП•(x)П•(xвҖІ)вҠӨwith П•: XвҶ’ RCГ—K, the scalability of the whole workп¬Ӯow can be unleashed. Concretely, letting П•X вҲҲRNCГ—K be the concatenation of {П•(xi) вҲҲRCГ—K}N i=1, we have (detailed derivation in Appendix A.2; see also [9]) ОәLLA(x,xвҖІ) вүҲПғ2 0 ( П•(x)П•(xвҖІ)вҠӨвҲ’П•(x)П•вҠӨ X [ ОӣвҲ’1 X,Y/Пғ2 0 + П•XП•вҠӨ X ]вҲ’1 П•XП•(xвҖІ)вҠӨ ) =П•(x) [вҲ‘ i П•(xi)вҠӨОӣ(xi,yi)П•(xi) + IK/Пғ2 0 оҙҷ оҙҳоҙ— оҙҡ G ]вҲ’1 П•(xвҖІ)вҠӨвүң ОәELLA(x,xвҖІ). (6) The matrix G вҲҲRKГ—K possesses a similar formula to the ОЈ in the original ОәLLA in Equation (3), yet much smaller. Once having П•, it is only required to perform one forward pass of gand П•for each training data to estimate G. When Kis reasonably small, e.g., <100, it is cheap to invert G. 3.3 Approximate NTKs via the NystrГ¶m Method Typical kernel approximation means include NystrГ¶m method [45, 58] and random features-based methods [48, 49, 61, 43, 15, 9]. Discussion In this work, the developed NystrГ¶m approximation to NTKs is mainly used for accel- erating the computation of the predictive covariance of LLA, while it can also be easily applied to future work on the practical application of NTKs. 3.4 Implementation As shown, the estimation of П•on a data point xdegenerates as KJVPs, which can be accomplished by invoking fwAD for Ktimes: ( x, ЛҶОё vk ) forward вҲ’вҲ’вҲ’вҲ’вҲ’вҲ’вҲ’вҲ’вҲ’вҲ’вҲ’вҶ’gЛҶОё(x) JЛҶОё(x)vk , kвҲҲ[K], (17) where the model output gЛҶОё(x) and the JVP JЛҶОё(x)vk are simultaneously computed in one single forward pass. Prevalent DL libraries like PyTorch [47] and Jax [2] have already been armed with the capability for fwAD. Algorithm 2 details the procedure of building П•in a PyTorch-like style. With that, we can trivially compute the posterior q(f) according to Equation (6), as detailed in Algorithm 1. The procedures for estimating G and the posterior can both be batched for acceleration. Computation overhead The estimation of П•involves M forward and backward passes of gЛҶОё and the eigendecomposition of a matrix of sizeMГ—M. After that, we only need to make {vk}K k=1 persistent, which amounts to storing K more NN copies. Estimating GвҲ’1 requires scanning the training set once and inverting a matrix of KГ—K, which is cheap. The evaluation of q(f) embodies that of П•, i.e., performing Kforward passes under the scope of fwAD. ELLA (or LLA) п¬Ғnds a GP posterior, so predicts with a variant of the aforementioned pos- terior predictive, formulated as p(y|xвҲ—,D) = Ep(f|D)p(y|f(xвҲ—)) вүҲ Eq(f)p(y|f(xвҲ—)) = EfвҲјN(gЛҶОё(xвҲ—),ОәELLA(xвҲ—,xвҲ—))p(y|f).In classiп¬Ғcation tasks, we use 512 MC samples to approximate the last expectation and it is cheap. 5.2 The Overп¬Ғtting Issue of LLA Reinspecting Equation (1) and (6), we see, with more training data involved, the covariance in LA, LLA, and ELLA shrinks and the uncertainty dissipates. However, under ubiquitous model misspeciп¬Ғcation [41], should the uncertainty vanish that fast? We perform a study with ResNets [18] on CIFAR-10 to seek an answer. Concretely, we randomly subsampleN data from the training set of CIFAR-10, and п¬Ғt ELLA on them. We depict the test negative log-likelihood (NLL), accuracy, and expected calibration error (ECE) [17] of the deployed ELLA in Figure 2 (c) and Appendix C.2. We also provide the corresponding results of LLA вҲ—4 in Appendix C.3. The V-shape NLL curves across settings reп¬Ӯect the overп¬Ғtting issue of ELLA and LLAвҲ—(or more generally, LLA). Figure 7 also shows that tuning the prior precision w.r.t. marginal likelihood can alleviate the overп¬Ғtting of LLAвҲ—to some extent, which may be the reason why such an issue has not been reported by previous works. But also of note that tuning the prior precision cannot fully eliminate overп¬Ғtting. To address the overп¬Ғtting issue, we advocate performing early stop when п¬Ғtting ELLA/LLA on big data. Compared to tuning the prior, early stopping also helps reduce the time cost of processing big data (e.g., ImageNet [8]). 5.3 Illustrative Regression We build a regression problem withN = 16 samples from y= sin 2x+Пө,Пө вҲјN(0,0.2) as shown in Figure 1. The model is an MLP with 3 hidden layers and tanh activations, and we pretrain it by MAP for 1000 iterations. For ELLA, we set M = 16 and K = 5 for efп¬Ғciency. Unless stated otherwise, we use the interfaces of Laplace [6] to implement LLA, LLA-KFAC, LLA-Diag, and LLAвҲ—. The hyperparameters of the competitors are equivalent to those of ELLA except for some dedicated ones like M and K. It is clear that ELLA delivers a closer approximation to LLA than LLA-Diag and LLAвҲ—. We further quantify the quality of the predictive distributions produced by these approximate LLA methods using certain metrics. Considering that in this case, the predictive distribution for one test datum is a Gaussian distribution, we use the KL divergence between the Gaussians yielded by the approximate LLA method and vanilla LLA as a proxy of the approximation error (averaged over a set of test points). The results are reported in Appendix C.4. As shown, LLA-KFAC comes pretty close to LLA. Yet, LLA seems to underestimate in-between uncertainty in this setting, so ELLA seems to be a more reliable (instead of more accurate) approximation than LLA-KFAC. We also highlight the higher scalability of ELLA than LLA-KFAC (see Figure 4(c)), which reп¬Ӯects that ELLA can strike a good trade-off between efп¬Ғcacy and efп¬Ғciency. 5.4 CIFAR-10 Classiп¬Ғcation Then, we evaluate ELLA on CIFAR-10 benchmark using ResNet architectures [ 18]. We obtain pretrained MAP models from the open source community. Table 2 presents the comparison on test accuracy, NLL, and ECE with ResNet architectures. We see that ELLA maintains its superiority in ECE while MFVI-BF can induce higher accuracy and lower NLL. We reveal by this experiment that ELLA can be a more applicable and scalable method than most of existing BNNs. 6 Related Work LA [38, 50, 14, 30, 22, 6, 7] locally approximates the Bayesian posterior with a Gaussian distribution, analogous to VI with Gaussian variationals [16, 1, 36, 52, 46] and SW AG [39]. LA can be applied to pretrained models effortlessly while the acquired posteriors are potentially restrictive (see Section 5.5). VI enjoys higher п¬Ӯexibility yet relies on costly training; BayesAdapter [10] seems to be a remedy to the issue but its accessibility is still lower than LA. SWAG stores a series of SGD iterates to heuristically construct an approximate posterior and is empirically weaker than LA/LLA [6]. Though more expressive approaches like deep ensembles [32] and MCMC [55, 4, 63] can explicitly explore diverse posterior modes, they face limitations in efп¬Ғciency and scalability. WhatвҖҷs more, it has been shown that LA/LLA can perform on par with or better than deep ensembles and cyclical MCMC on multiple benchmarks [6]. This may be attributed to the un-identiп¬Ғed, complicated relationships between the parameters space and the function space of DNNs [59]. Also of note that LA can embrace deep ensembles to capture multiple posterior modes [13]. [9] introduces a general kernel approximation technique using neural networks. By contrast, we focus on leveraging kernel approximation to accelerate Laplace approximation. Thus, the focus of the two works is distinct. Indeed, this work shares a similar idea with the Sec 4.3 in [ 9] that LLA can be accelerated by kernel approximation. But, except for such an idea, this work differentiates from [9] in aspects like motivations, techniques, implementations, theoretical backgrounds, and applications. Our implementation, theoretical analysis, and some empirical п¬Ғndings are all novel. 7 Conclustion This paper proposes ELLA, a simple, effective, and reliable approach for Bayesian deep learning. ELLA addresses the unreliability issues of existing approximations to LLA and is implemented based on NystrГ¶m method. We offer theoretical guarantees for ELLA and perform extensive studies to testify its efп¬Ғcacy and scalability. ELLA currently accounts for only the predictive, and extending it to estimate model evidence for model selection [38] deserves future investigation. Using model evidence to select the number of remaining eigenpairs Kfor ELLA is also viable. B How Does ELLA Address the Scalability Issues of NystrГ¶m Approximation Existing works like NeuralEF [9] highlight the scalability issues of the NystrГ¶m approximation when applied to NTKs, but they have been successfully addressed by ELLA, which reп¬Ӯects our technical novelty. On the one hand, the cost of eigendecomposing the M Г—M matrix in our case is low considering that we set M <104. As shown in Figure 2(a)(b), M = 2000 can lead to reasonably small approximation errors for both the NystrГ¶m approximation and the resulting ELLA covariance. On the other hand, the prediction cost at test time is reduced by two innovations in this work. Firstly, looking at Equation (14), typical NystrГ¶m method п¬Ғrst computes JЛҶОё(x,i)JвҠӨ ЛҶОё,ЛңX (i.e., evaluate the NTK similarities between (x,i) and all training data ЛңX) and then multiplies the result with uk вҲҲRM to get the evaluation of k-th eigenfunction. So it needs to store JЛҶОё,ЛңX вҲҲRMГ—P and compute M Jacobian-vector products (JVPs). But this work proposes to п¬Ғrst computevk = JвҠӨ ЛҶОё,ЛңXuk,k = 1,...,K (we omit the scalar multiplier) and then obtain the evaluation of k-th eigenfunction by JЛҶОё(x,i)vk. Namely, we only need to store Kvectors of size P and compute KJVPs. In most cases, M = 2000 and K = 20, so the beneп¬Ғts of our technique are obvious. Secondly, we leverage forward-mode 17Table 4: Test NLL of ELLA varies w.r.t.M and K(M вүҘK) on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-20 architecture. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: The paper addresses the scalability and fidelity issues of existing Linearized Laplace Approximation (LLA) methods in Bayesian deep learning. It introduces an Accelerated LLA (ELLA) that uses a NystrГ¶m approximation of the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) to efficiently compute the predictive posterior. This approach avoids explicit computation of large GGN or Jacobian matrices, scales to modern architectures like Vision Transformers, and comes with theoretical guarantees on its approximation error. The paper also identifies and provides a solution (early stopping) for an overfitting issue in LLA on large datasets.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: The method, named ELLA (accElerated Linearized Laplace Approximation), reformulates the LLA predictive posterior through its connection to Neural Tangent Kernels (NTKs). To avoid the costly computation and inversion of the full NTK Gram matrix, it employs the NystrГ¶m method to find a low-rank approximation. This involves constructing a feature map based on the top-K eigenfunctions of the NTK, estimated from a subset of M data points. The implementation is made efficient by using forward-mode automatic differentiation (fwAD) to compute the required Jacobian-vector products (JVPs) without explicitly forming the Jacobian matrix.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: The method was evaluated on a 1D regression task and image classification benchmarks including CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. Robustness was tested on out-of-distribution datasets like CIFAR-10/ImageNet corruptions and SVHN. A variety of neural network architectures were used, including MLPs, ResNets of different depths, and Vision Transformers (ViT-B). ELLA was compared against baselines such as MAP, Mean-Field Variational Inference (MFVI-BF), and various LLA approximations (last-layer, diagonal, KFAC). Evaluation metrics included test accuracy, Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL), and Expected Calibration Error (ECE).
llm_extracted_info.limitations: The paper identifies that LLA and ELLA can suffer from an overfitting issue when applied to large datasets, where predictive uncertainty diminishes too rapidly, leading to increased NLL. The proposed solution is a practical heuristic (early stopping). The performance of ELLA is dependent on the quality of the initial MAP-trained model, as it only adjusts the covariance of the posterior, not the mean. The choice of hyperparameters M (number of NystrГ¶m samples) and K (number of eigenpairs) requires empirical tuning.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: The authors suggest extending ELLA to estimate the model evidence, which is useful for model selection. A related direction is to use this model evidence to automatically select the hyperparameter K, the number of eigenpairs retained in the NystrГ¶m approximation.
